summary: Deep adversarial + constructive red-team of meldingx v0.2: attack hard, then propose creative soul-preserving alternative routes
logs: Analyze done: 9 dimensions, 56 attacks (30 high/critical), 63 constructive alternatives proposed

analyses.dimension: ANTI-ABUSE — Anonymous anti-abuse / flood resistance on a serverless BT DTN
analyses.assessment: The spec is admirably honest: it already concedes PoW is a speed-bump and names anonymous rate-limiting as the central unsolved problem. So the real review question is not "is PoW broken" (it is, and you say so) but "is the design's structure compatible with ANY anonymous rate-limiter, and which candidate actually survives the no-infrastructure + partition-prone + battery-asymmetric environment?" My finding: the two candidates you name (RLN, blind-signed tokens) both quietly import infrastructure that the rest of the spec forbids. RLN needs (a) a shared, agreed membership Merkle tree, (b) synchronized clocks/epochs, and (c) an economic stake to slash — all three are documented requirements and all three are exactly what a no-server, partition-prone, identity-free mesh cannot provide. Blind tokens need a persistent online issuer. The deeper structural problem is that EVERY anonymous-rate-limiter is two mechanisms welded together: a Sybil-resistant ENROLLMENT gate (so one human can't mint unlimited identities) and a double-use DETECTION substrate (so over-rate is caught). meldingx has a genuinely good, under-exploited asset for the first — out-of-band, in-person pairing is itself a strong proof-of-personhood/rate-limited-mint event — and a genuinely hostile environment for the second, because detection requires the two conflicting proofs to CONVERGE in one observer's buffer, and your own §11 says buffers hold <1% of a metropolis soup and partitions are normal. The most important architectural correction: stop trying to bound "messages per global identity" (impossible without a global view) and instead bound "messages a NEIGHBOR will RELAY for you per window" using link-local, identity-free credentials that are spent into the link, not the soup. That keeps the cost on the receive path (which you already identify as the real cost) and never needs global convergence. PoW's correct residual role is not admission but as the keying material for the outer-frame MAC and as a memory-hard equalizer that you should reframe around the verify-cheap/forge-expensive asymmetry, not raw compute.

attacks.title: RLN's three hidden dependencies are all forbidden by your own invariants
attacks.mechanism: RLN (as deployed in Waku) requires: (1) a single agreed membership Merkle tree of identity commitments — in Waku this lives in a smart contract precisely because peers must agree on it; (2) synchronized clocks, because the rate rule is 'X proofs per epoch' and the per-epoch external nullifier is Poseidon(epoch, rln_id) — desync = you cannot tell a re-use from a new epoch; (3) an economic stake to slash, which is the ONLY thing that makes the Shamir-reveal a deterrent. meldingx has no server to host the tree, no global clock in a blackout/partition, and no stake (no accounts, no money, no identity on the wire). Bolting RLN on either re-introduces a server (kills invariant 1/6) or degrades to a tree that different partitions disagree on — at which point an attacker simply operates in a partition whose tree doesn't contain the slashing observers, and re-uses keys freely.
attacks.breaks: Invariants 1 (no internet), 2 (no infra/no routing-style global state), 6 (no server/account)

attacks.title: Detection requires convergence; your own scaling math guarantees non-convergence
attacks.mechanism: Both RLN (Shamir reveal) and offline e-cash (double-spend reveal at deposit) only catch an abuser when the TWO conflicting proofs land in the SAME observer's view. In a soup where every node holds <1% of live blobs (§11) and the network partitions constantly (gathering scope, BLE range), an attacker can deterministically avoid convergence: emit each over-rate copy into a different spatial cell / different partition, or simply rely on eviction to drop one of the two shares before they meet. The math that makes flooding cap scale (§11) is the SAME math that makes any convergence-based abuse detector blind. So the headline candidate primitives don't just lack infrastructure — they lack the global view their security proof assumes.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 2 (pure flooding / no global state) co-defeats the only abuse detectors that fit invariant 4

attacks.title: Congestion-coupled PoW is a remote battery-drain / self-DoS amplifier
attacks.mechanism: §8 couples PoW difficulty to LOCAL congestion (≈0 when calm, rises under attack). An attacker injects a burst of cheap-to-him (GPU/ASIC) high-difficulty blobs to spike every nearby honest phone's PERCEIVED congestion, forcing honest originators to mine harder on battery while the attacker pays mains-power rates. Worse: the difficulty signal is itself attacker-controlled (relays are the adversary, §6), so the attacker can lie 'congestion is high' to make the weakest battery phone the throttle point — which §8 admits is the defender. The feedback loop is a remote knob on victims' CPUs.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 1 (full function in blackout — dead batteries = dead mesh) and the defender=weakest-phone reality

attacks.title: Pre-mined PoW pool decouples cost from rate — defeating PoW's only honest job
attacks.mechanism: §10 says pre-mine PoW off the send path so origination causes no timing/power tell. But a pre-mined pool means the marginal cost of sending the Nth message in a burst is ~0 (you spend stored solutions). PoW only deters floods if it's paid per-message at send time. An attacker pre-mines a giant pool at leisure on mains power, then floods instantly. You've optimized away the anonymity tell AND the rate deterrent simultaneously — the pool converts PoW from a (weak) flow limiter into a pure one-time admission tax that amortizes to zero over a flood.
attacks.breaks: Anti-flood goal; the anonymity fix and the abuse fix are in direct tension here

attacks.title: Cover traffic is an attacker's free flood amplifier and accounting blind spot
attacks.mechanism: Invariant 3 requires real msgs to be indistinguishable from cover, and §10 says cover blobs 'propagate exactly like real messages' and are first-emission, PoW-valid, sealed-to-null. That means honest nodes MUST carry and re-broadcast cover indistinguishably — so an attacker mass-produces 'cover-shaped' blobs and the network is structurally forbidden from dropping them (dropping them differentially would break uniformity). Cover traffic is thus a sanctioned, undetectable flood vector: the more uniformity you enforce, the less you can rate-limit, because any per-blob scrutiny that could throttle the attacker also distinguishes real from cover.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 3 (uniformity) is in direct, structural tension with any anti-flood discrimination

attacks.title: Eviction 'attacker-uncorrelated' is hard to keep uncorrelated under adaptive flooding
attacks.mechanism: §8 says never evict least-PoW (correct) and use randomized + per-neighbor share cap (≤15%) + retain-closest-to-delivery. But 'closest-to-delivery' has no meaning without routing (invariant 2) — there's no delivery gradient. So eviction reduces to randomized + per-neighbor-cap. An attacker spread across K physical relays (K phones in the crowd) each stays under 15%, collectively occupying up to ~K×15% before the cap bites, and randomized eviction then drops honest and attack blobs proportionally — meaning a 10x flood evicts ~90% of honest messages purely by dilution, with no policy violation. Randomized eviction is target-unsteerable (good) but not flood-resistant (the buffer still fills with attacker soup and honest delivery craters).
attacks.breaks: Delivery under flood; the per-neighbor cap doesn't compose across many attacker radios

attacks.title: OOB-pairing as Sybil gate is bypassed by self-pairing / pairing farms
attacks.mechanism: If you (as I recommend) use in-person OOB pairing to mint rate-limited relay credentials, an attacker pairs with their own 1000 phones (or runs a pairing farm), each pairing minting credentials. There's no personhood check in 'scan my own QR.' The gate raises the cost from ~0 to 'physical device + a scan,' which is meaningful against a casual flooder but a state actor with 1000 phones in a stadium gets 1000× quota. Without proof-of-personhood (which needs either biometrics or a trusted festival of strangers — see BrightID/pseudonym-parties), in-person pairing bounds Sybils only by device count, not human count.
attacks.breaks: Anti-Sybil basis of any credential-issued rate limit; bends invariant 6 if you try to fix with accounts

attacks.title: Seen-record (Bloom) is a poisoning and false-positive censorship target
attacks.mechanism: The 30-day Bloom seen-record hard-drops any ID it thinks it has seen. Bloom filters have one-sided error: false POSITIVES. An attacker who can predict or grind message-IDs (or just floods enough distinct IDs to saturate the filter's load factor) raises the global FP rate, causing honest never-seen messages to be silently dropped as 'already seen' — a stealthy censorship/DoS that looks like normal loss. Because the filter is per-node and append-only for 30 days, a sustained flood degrades every node's filter into a higher-FP state with no recovery short of reset.
attacks.breaks: Delivery integrity; anti-replay machinery becomes an availability liability under flood

constructiveAlternatives.title: Link-local relay credentials: rate-limit RELAYING, not ORIGINATION (the keystone idea)
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: RLN/e-cash convergence-blindness; pre-mined PoW; eviction-under-flood; defender=weakest-phone
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Stop trying to bound 'messages per global identity' (needs a global view you can never have under invariant 2). Instead, each phone enforces a purely LOCAL, identity-free rule: 'I will accept-and-relay at most R blobs per neighbor per epoch, and to spend one of my R slots you must present a fresh, unlinkable single-use token that I can verify cheaply but that was expensive to acquire.' The token is spent INTO THE LINK (consumed in the BLE handshake before full transfer), never embedded in the blob, so it leaves no on-wire metadata and the soup stays uniform. Detection of over-rate is now trivial and LOCAL: each neighbor counts tokens it has seen this epoch — no convergence, no global tree, no synchronized global clock (epoch = neighbor-local rolling window). This is the standard DTN defense (per-source rate limit) made anonymous by replacing 'source identity' with 'unlinkable one-time token,' and made convergence-free by scoping it to the one hop you can actually observe.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Puts the cost exactly on the receive path you already named as the real cost (§8); needs zero infrastructure, zero global clock, zero global tree; tokens carry no identity so sealed-sender and uniformity hold; a flood now costs the attacker one scarce token PER HOP PER NEIGHBOR, which compounds against propagation instead of amortizing like a pre-mined origination pool.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Token issuance still needs a Sybil-resistant source (see next two items); verification adds a few ms per neighbor handshake; honest multi-hop messages spend more tokens than single-hop, so token economics must be calibrated so honest reach is affordable.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Mint relay tokens at OOB pairing + via offline-e-cash-style self-issued wallets
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Where do the link-local tokens come from with no issuer? (the enrollment gate)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Two stacked sources, both infra-free. (A) PAIRING MINT: every in-person OOB pairing (already in your design) mints a batch of blind-signed relay tokens co-signed by BOTH parties' keys — a 'we met' attestation. This ties token supply to real-world social events, exactly the BrightID/pseudonym-party intuition for Sybil resistance, but fully offline and 2-party. (B) SELF-MINTED PoSW WALLET: a phone can also mint its own tokens by burning a Verifiable Delay Function / proof-of-sequential-work (VDF) — non-parallelizable, so 1000 phones still each need wall-clock time per token and an ASIC gives NO speedup (unlike PoW). The token = (VDF output serial, self-signature). Over-issuance is bounded by the device's single sequential thread × wall-clock, which is the closest thing to 'proof of one human's elapsed time' available with no server. Neighbors accept either token type; the VDF path guarantees the mesh works in a pure-stranger blackout with zero prior pairings.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Pairing-minted tokens make the social graph (which you already build OOB) into the Sybil-resistance substrate without putting it on the wire; VDF tokens give a no-prior-contact fallback that ASICs/GPUs cannot accelerate (defeating PoW's core weakness from §8); both are verify-cheap so the weakest battery phone only VERIFIES, never mines, at relay time.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: VDF on a phone is power-hungry to MINT (but done off the send path, at idle/charging — acceptable as it's the attacker-equalizer, not the hot path); pairing-mint requires the pairing UX to also exchange a token batch; a 1000-phone farm still gets 1000 VDF streams (bounds Sybils by device-count, not human-count — honest mitigation, not a cure).

constructiveAlternatives.title: Shamir-reveal 'self-incrimination' tokens — RLN's idea WITHOUT the tree/stake/clock
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: RLN's three forbidden dependencies; making over-rate self-punishing locally
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Keep RLN's beautiful core — a key split by Shamir so that re-use above the limit reconstructs the secret — but drop the membership tree, the stake, and the global epoch. Construction: when a phone mints a relay token (via VDF or pairing), it embeds a per-token Shamir polynomial whose constant term is a self-penalty seed bound to THAT TOKEN ONLY (not a global identity). The link-local rule is 'one share per token per neighbor-epoch.' If an attacker re-spends the SAME token to one neighbor twice, that neighbor reconstructs the seed and can locally blacklist the token AND broadcast a compact 'this token is burned' note (an ordinary blob) so other neighbors drop it too. Crucially the penalty is token-scoped, so it reveals NO identity (sealed-sender intact) — it just kills the abused token. This needs no global tree (the token carries its own commitment), no stake (the penalty is loss of the scarce token), and only a NEIGHBOR-LOCAL epoch.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Recovers RLN's elegant 'abuse is self-revealing' property while shedding every piece of infrastructure RLN normally requires; the revealed quantity is a token, not a person, so anonymity is untouched; burned-token notes propagate as ordinary uniform blobs.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Only punishes re-use of the SAME token at the SAME neighbor (cross-neighbor re-spend still needs the token-supply cap to bind); ZK proof of correct share generation adds proof size/verify cost — must be benchmarked on a low-end phone; if tokens are abundant, burning one is cheap, so it complements but does not replace supply scarcity.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Storage-proof relay reciprocity: pay to inject by proving you carry others' soup
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Relay-retention tragedy-of-the-commons (§13.2) AND flood cost simultaneously
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Make the right to INJECT a new blob contingent on a cheap-to-verify proof that you are currently STORING a sampled set of OTHER nodes' live blobs (a proof-of-retrievability / proof-of-space-time over your buffer). At each neighbor handshake, the neighbor issues a random challenge over the blob-IDs it knows you should be holding (from the have/want Bloom gossip) and you must answer with the requested bytes/Merkle openings. Passing earns you a small, short-lived relay allowance from that neighbor; failing throttles you. This binds 'I get to use the commons' to 'I carry the commons,' turning the §6 Knob-2 incentive problem into a verifiable obligation — without identity, since the proof is over content you hold, not who you are.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Directly attacks the free-rider/tragedy problem AND raises flood cost (a flooder who refuses to store others' soup gets throttled; one who DOES store it is spending real device resources proportional to network size); verification is asymmetric — checking a few openings is cheap for the weak phone, faking large storage is expensive for the attacker (the proof-of-space asymmetry from the literature).
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Adds challenge-response bytes to each handshake; a wealthy attacker with lots of storage can pass and still flood (caps the abuse at their real storage cost, doesn't zero it); interacts with privacy — challenge patterns must be randomized so they don't fingerprint what you hold.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Two-tier soup: a high-trust 'paired-contact lane' immune to stranger floods
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Flood dilution of honest delivery; eviction-under-flood; the fact that real users mostly message known contacts
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Split the buffer into two pools with separate eviction. POOL A ('contact lane'): blobs that arrived bearing a valid pairing-minted relay token from someone in (or transitively near) your OOB social graph get a reserved, separate buffer quota that stranger traffic CANNOT evict. POOL B ('open soup'): everything else (VDF-token traffic, cover, pure strangers) shares the remaining buffer and absorbs floods. Because real messages between friends ride Pool A and floods land in Pool B, a stranger flood degrades only Pool B; your friend's message survives. The token reveals graph-PROXIMITY, not identity, and only to the locally-deciding node (never on the wire to others), so sealed-sender and uniformity hold for everyone else.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Gives honest contact-to-contact delivery a flood-immune reservation — the single biggest practical robustness win — while keeping the open stranger soup for blackout/first-contact use; eviction in Pool A can be near-FIFO-by-TTL since it's small and trusted.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: BENDS invariant 3/4 SLIGHTLY: a node's LOCAL decision to file a blob in Pool A leaks, to that one node only, 'this token came from my graph' — it does not go on the wire and others can't see it, but it is a local distinguisher between 'mine-adjacent' and 'stranger' traffic. Soul-preserving fallback: keep a single uniform pool but give pairing-token traffic a HIGHER eviction-survival WEIGHT in the randomized eviction lottery (probabilistic, not a separate visible lane) — strictly less leakage, slightly weaker guarantee.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Reframe PoW as a memory-hard, verify-cheap EQUALIZER + outer-MAC key, not an admission tax
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: PoW battery-asymmetry; congestion-coupling self-DoS; pre-mined-pool amortization
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Three fixes to the PoW you keep: (1) Use a memory-HARD, memory-BOUND function where VERIFICATION needs far less memory than GENERATION (the proof-of-space asymmetry) — this compresses the GPU/ASIC advantage far more than Argon2 alone, because bandwidth/latency, not FLOPs, is the bottleneck and phones-vs-ASICs are closer on memory bandwidth than on compute. (2) DROP congestion-coupling as a difficulty knob (it's an attacker-controlled remote CPU knob, see attacks); replace with a FIXED floor difficulty plus the VDF token for rate. (3) Keep PoW ONLY as (a) the key for the non-identifying outer-frame MAC (§10) and (b) a one-time per-blob anti-accident tax — and explicitly DO NOT pre-mine if you want it to deter floods; instead move the rate-deterrence entirely to the per-hop tokens, which DON'T amortize.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Removes the remote-battery-drain amplifier; closes the pre-mine amortization hole by relocating rate-deterrence to non-amortizable per-hop tokens; the memory-bound choice narrows the attacker's edge from 10^4-10^8 (your §8 figure) toward 10^1-10^2, which is the difference between 'useless' and 'meaningful speed bump.'
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Memory-hard verify still costs the weak phone more than a hashcheck (must benchmark); losing congestion-coupling means difficulty can't auto-rise — you rely on tokens for surge defense; needs careful constant-time MAC to avoid a new side channel.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Gossip-converged double-spend evidence as a slow, best-effort second layer
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Catching cross-neighbor token re-use that the link-local cap misses
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Accept that convergence is unreliable (per attacks) and use it only as a BONUS layer, not the primary defense. When any node DOES happen to see two valid spends of the same token serial (the offline-e-cash deposit moment), it can reconstruct the Shamir penalty and emit a tiny 'burned serial' blob into the soup. These propagate like ordinary blobs and let the network probabilistically converge on burning abused tokens over time. Treat it as eventually-consistent reputation FOR TOKENS (never for people): high-churn serials get burned where the network is dense, with graceful failure where it's partitioned (the link-local cap still holds locally regardless).
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Salvages the one genuinely useful property of RLN/e-cash (re-use is cryptographically self-incriminating) as an opportunistic enhancement that is SAFE to fail — when it can't converge you lose nothing beyond the link-local floor; burned-serial blobs are uniform and identity-free.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Probabilistic and slow; useless in a fresh partition (which is when you may need it most); adds a small class of blobs to carry; must rate-limit the burn-notes themselves or they become a flood vector.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Density-adaptive token pricing: cheap when sparse, scarce when dense
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Sparse/blackout reach (you must NOT throttle when the mesh is starving) vs flood defense when dense
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Each node sets the relay-token PRICE it demands from neighbors as a function of LOCALLY-OBSERVED neighbor density and buffer pressure — not a global congestion signal (which is forgeable). In a sparse 2-neighbor blackout cell, demanded price ~0 (relay everything, reach is precious). In a packed stadium with a full buffer, price rises so each neighbor must spend more tokens to inject, throttling floods exactly where they're possible. Because the price is set per-node from first-hand observation (how many radios do I actually hear, how full is MY buffer), it can't be remotely spiked by a lying relay the way §8's congestion-coupled PoW can.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Resolves the central tension between 'relay generously when sparse' (invariant 7 / reach) and 'throttle when dense' (flood defense); uses only first-hand local observation so it's not a remote-controllable knob; degrades to free relaying in exactly the blackout case ranked #1.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Local density is itself somewhat gameable (attacker can present many radios to look dense and raise price to suppress honest traffic — though that costs the attacker tokens too); pricing curve needs field tuning; must ensure cover-traffic floor is exempt so uniformity holds at any price.

creativeIdeas: Treat the OOB social graph you ALREADY build as the system's only real Sybil-resistance budget, and spend it deliberately: transitive 'we-met' attestations (A paired B, B paired C) let A's traffic earn a tiny survival bonus in C's eviction lottery without ever revealing A or the path — a blinded, hop-limited web-of-trust that lives entirely off-wire.

Make COVER traffic do double duty as a flood SINK: define cover blobs to be the FIRST thing evicted under buffer pressure (a node knows its OWN cover is fake even if others can't), so honest nodes self-prune their fakes before dropping real soup — uniformity on the WIRE is preserved (others can't tell), but each node has a private flood-absorption reservoir.

A 'proof of having waited' instead of 'proof of having computed': a VDF seeded by a recent, network-observable beacon (e.g. a rolling hash of recently-seen blob-IDs) means a token can't be pre-mined before that beacon existed — killing the pre-mine amortization attack at its root while staying serverless (the beacon is emergent from the soup itself).

Per-neighbor 'invalid-traffic credit score' that is purely local and ephemeral: a neighbor who sends mostly-failing PoW/token handshakes gets fast-dropped (already in §8) — extend it so the DROP DECISION is randomized in timing so it can't be probed, and resets on disconnect so it stores no durable per-peer state (no identity, no memory).

Honeypot serials for floods: nodes occasionally MINT decoy tokens designed to be attractive to a token-harvesting attacker but whose re-use trivially reconstructs the burn-seed — turning an attacker's own greed into self-incrimination, RLN's spirit weaponized as a trap rather than a tax.

Embrace 'good-enough at gathering scale': formally state the anti-abuse goal as 'an attacker cannot deny service to friend-to-friend (Pool A) traffic in a dense gathering without physically deploying O(crowd-size) radios,' which is an ACHIEVABLE, falsifiable target — far more defensible than 'stop a state flooder,' which §8 already concedes is impossible.

Two-clock epochs: never rely on a global clock; let each neighbor pair establish a SHARED epoch at connection time from a fresh nonce exchange, so 'one token per epoch per neighbor' needs zero global time sync and is immune to the clock-desync attacks that break textbook RLN.

techniques.name: RLN (Rate-Limiting Nullifier) — Semaphore + Shamir secret sharing, Waku/PSE
techniques.relevance: The canonical anonymous rate-limiter. Borrow the 'over-rate reconstructs a penalty secret' core; but verified from the docs: it REQUIRES an agreed membership Merkle tree (on-chain in Waku), synchronized clocks/epochs, and an economic stake to slash — all three are infeasible on a serverless partition-prone DTN. Use the Shamir idea token-scoped (not identity-scoped), with neighbor-local epochs.

techniques.name: Brands / Chaum offline e-cash with double-spend deanonymization
techniques.relevance: Structurally identical to your problem: anonymous unlinkable mint, and re-use (double-spend) cryptographically reveals a penalty AT DEPOSIT. The catch — confirmed in the literature — is that detection happens 'when tokens are deposited at the bank,' i.e. at a convergence point. On a mesh, the soup is the imperfect 'bank' and convergence is unreliable; treat double-spend reveal as a best-effort second layer, not the primary defense.

techniques.name: Privacy Pass / ARC (Anonymous Rate-Limited Credentials), IETF privacypass WG
techniques.relevance: Blind-signed, unlinkable, multi-use-up-to-k tokens — exactly the 'per-epoch mint token' candidate in your §8. Cloudflare's 2025 deployment confirms the shape. But it needs a persistent online ISSUER and per-origin presentation contexts; the buildable adaptation for meldingx is to replace the issuer with (a) OOB pairing co-signatures and (b) self-issued VDF tokens, and replace 'per-origin' with 'per-neighbor link.'

techniques.name: Verifiable Delay Functions / Proof of Sequential Work (Wesolowski, Pietrzak; Boneh et al.)
techniques.relevance: The ONE primitive that fits 'no infrastructure' cleanly: non-parallelizable, ASIC gives ~no speedup (directly defeats your §8 10^4-10^8 PoW gap), verify-cheap. Use as the serverless, stranger-friendly token mint. Caveat: it caps rate PER SEQUENTIAL THREAD, so N devices still get N× — bounds Sybils by device count, not human count.

techniques.name: Proofs of Space / Proof-of-Space-Time / Proof-of-Retrievability
techniques.relevance: Asymmetric verify-cheap / forge-expensive (confirmed in the literature for mobile-vs-powerful-device settings). Two uses: (1) a memory-BOUND replacement for hashcash PoW that narrows the attacker edge because phones and ASICs are closer on memory bandwidth than on FLOPs; (2) the storage-proof relay-reciprocity scheme that ties 'right to inject' to 'verifiably carrying others' soup,' attacking your tragedy-of-the-commons open question.

techniques.name: BrightID / Proof-of-Humanity / pseudonym-parties (Ford et al.)
techniques.relevance: Sybil resistance from in-person social events and vouching — the closest real-world analog to using your OOB pairing as a personhood gate. The honest limit (from the literature) is that without biometrics or a synchronous in-person 'party,' you bound Sybils by device/connection count, not by humans; set expectations accordingly rather than claiming Sybil-proofness.

techniques.name: Briar / Bramble transport + Tor rendezvous
techniques.relevance: You already cite it (§14). For anti-abuse specifically, Bramble's per-link flow control and contact-graph-gated relaying are a working precedent for 'rate-limit at the link using the OOB contact graph' — strong evidence the link-local + pairing-graph approach I recommend is implementable, not speculative.

techniques.name: Sybil/clock-sync attack literature on epoch systems (Douceur Sybil; PTP/IEEE-1588 time-sync attacks)
techniques.relevance: Backs the attack that RLN's synchronized-clock requirement is a real, exploitable dependency, and motivates the 'two-clock per-neighbor epoch from a fresh nonce' fix that needs no global time. Mark as supporting rationale, not a meldingx-specific result.

analyses.dimension: Anonymity & traffic-analysis under pure flooding (uniform soup)
analyses.assessment: The uniformity claim is the load-bearing pillar of the whole design, and it is *half* true. The spec is genuinely strong on the part everyone gets wrong (content, sealed-sender, no identity-on-wire, MAC demotion, cover-as-first-emission, charge-independent floor). Where it is weak is the part it half-admits and then under-defends: uniformity is asserted at the level of a single blob ("real blob == cover blob == relayed blob") but anonymity is actually broken at the level of *processes over time* — first-emergence/causal-provenance, statistical disclosure across repeated sessions, and PHY identity that makes those time-series trivially partitionable per handset. The spec's own threat table concedes the "persistent wide-coverage adversary" is undefended and treats that as an edge case; but the focus dimension is exactly that adversary, and against it the per-blob uniformity argument does almost no work. The core unexamined gap: a real blob and a cover blob may be byte-identical, but the *event of a blob first existing in the world* is not uniform — its first appearance is spatially and temporally anchored to a transmitter, and the design's own "cover = first-emission dummies that propagate like real messages" is what makes first-emergence a usable signal rather than neutralizing it (dummies give you cover for the *fact* of originating, but a dummy and a real message are distinguished by whether the originator ever cares about an ACK, ever re-mints, ever shows diurnal correlation with a partner). The biggest single soundness problem is that the spec never quantifies the real:cover ratio or the k-anonymity set it buys, while simultaneously requiring cover to "match bursty/diurnal human distribution" — those two goals are in tension, and the design has no mechanism that resolves it. Net: the design is sound against the one-shot co-located sniffer it scopes for, plausibly sound against a non-global SDA adversary if (and only if) the cover model and a first-emergence muddle are added, and currently unsound against the persistent sensor net it names as out-of-scope but that the focus area demands we confront.

attacks.title: First-emergence / first-spy provenance: pure flooding does NOT hide the originator from a multi-sensor adversary
attacks.mechanism: The spec frames 'no routing => no wavefront => no source.' This is false against multiple co-located/blanket sensors. In epidemic flooding the originator is the unique node that emits a given message-ID at time T0 with NO prior reception of it. The Dandelion++ / rumor-source literature formalizes this as the 'first-spy estimator': the first honest-or-adversarial node to *observe* a novel ID, working backward, is a near-sufficient statistic for the source. With a sensor mesh covering a venue (cheap: a dozen Raspberry-Pi+BLE sniffers around a stadium concourse), the adversary timestamps the first sighting of every distinct message-ID and triangulates by signal strength to the phone that emitted it before anyone else did. Cover traffic does NOT fix this — it only means the adversary localizes the origin of *every* blob, real and dummy alike; it does not reduce the per-blob localization. To hide WHICH origin is real you still need the real/cover distinction to be unbreakable downstream (it is not — see attacks below).
attacks.breaks: Invariant 2 (no path/source metadata) and Invariant 3 (uniformity) — the *origination event* is metadata that flooding does not erase; uniformity holds for the blob but not for the act of first-emission.

attacks.title: Statistical disclosure / intersection attack across repeated gatherings deanonymizes the friend-pair
attacks.mechanism: Sealed-sender hides who/whom in any single blob, but the two friends co-locate (or co-attend) repeatedly. A persistent adversary with PHY-fingerprinting (so it can re-identify each handset across days regardless of MAC rotation) runs the classic Statistical Disclosure Attack (Danezis 2003; Mathewson-Danezis 'Practical Traffic Analysis'). It does not need to read anything: it correlates (a) which two handsets are co-present when a novel real-looking ID first emerges and an ACK-shaped reply later emerges, (b) diurnal/bursty send patterns the spec EXPLICITLY tells cover traffic to imitate, (c) the active-delete event (§7) which fires only for real messages. Over N gatherings the intersection of 'phones present when A originated AND a reply emerged' collapses to the A-B pair. The spec's own §10 'match bursty/diurnal human distribution' is the very behavioral signature SDA exploits — making cover look human makes the human-pair correlation re-usable as the fingerprint.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 3 (uniformity over time) and Invariant 4 (sealed-sender) — the pair relationship leaks through co-presence + timing even though no single blob names anyone.

attacks.title: PHY-layer fingerprinting collapses the anonymity set to one handset, turning every other tell into a labeled time-series
attacks.mechanism: The spec assumes the SDR/PHY adversary 'still only yields you-emit-blobs.' That understates it badly. RF fingerprinting of BLE is real and cheap: published work (e.g. ACM SenSys 2021 'Identifying BLE Devices'; multiple arXiv RFFI papers 2024-2025) extracts carrier-frequency-offset + I/Q transient signatures that uniquely label a *physical radio* across MAC and payload rotation, ~84-99% accuracy with a commodity USRP. Once every transmission is labeled with a stable hardware identity, MAC randomization, payload rotation, and ID-free advertisements are ALL moot for linkage — they only stop a cheap passive sniffer, which the spec concedes. The danger is compositional: PHY-fingerprint provides the stable axis; first-emergence provides per-blob origin; SDA provides pair-linkage. Each is survivable alone; together they reconstruct the social graph of everyone who runs the app at a gathering, without decrypting a byte.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 3 (uniformity) at the device level — uniformity of *payload* is irrelevant once the *transmitter* is uniquely labeled.

attacks.title: PoW pre-mining pool depletion is itself an origination timing tell
attacks.mechanism: §10 pre-mines PoW off the send path so origination causes no CPU/power spike — good. But the pool is finite and difficulty is 'coupled to local congestion.' Under attack/high congestion, difficulty rises; a phone that ORIGINATES real messages drains its pre-mined pool faster than a pure relay (relays don't spend PoW; they forward existing valid PoW). An adversary that can induce congestion (flood the venue) and then observe which handsets exhibit secondary CPU/thermal/power-draw spikes (re-mining to refill the pool) has a power-side-channel that fires only for actual originators. Pure relays never re-mine. This re-creates the very CPU-spike-on-origination tell the pre-mine was meant to kill, just time-shifted to refill.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 3 (uniformity) — re-mining work is a side channel that distinguishes originator from relay.

attacks.title: Cover traffic that is NOT receiver-bound is defeated by the standard SDA result
attacks.mechanism: §10 cover = 'sealed-to-null' first-emission dummies. The anonymity literature is explicit (Mallesh-Wright, 'Countering Statistical Disclosure with Receiver-Bound Cover Traffic,' 2007) that *sender-generated cover addressed to nobody* provides far weaker protection against SDA than cover that is statistically indistinguishable from real receiver-bound traffic, because the adversary can sometimes exploit asymmetries (dummies never generate ACKs, never get actively-deleted, never trigger a recipient's disappear-after-read behavior). Sealed-to-null blobs are a population with ZERO downstream interaction events; real blobs are a population with nonzero ACK/delete/read-correlated events. The two populations are separable in aggregate even if individually byte-identical.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 3 (uniformity) — cover and real differ in their *downstream event distribution* even when identical on the wire.

attacks.title: Active-delete is a per-message 'this one was real' oracle even on the sender side
attacks.mechanism: The spec relocates the delete emitter to the sender + random delay (good, fixes the recipient leak). But it remains a binary signal that segregates the message population: a 'secret' broadcast PROVES the corresponding message-ID was a real, delivered, ACKed message — never a dummy. A persistent adversary harvests all 'secret' reveals, back-maps each to its message-ID's first-emergence event, and thereby gets a clean labeled training set of {real originations}. That labeled set is exactly what SDA/intersection needs to bootstrap. Random delay only decorrelates the delete from the read; it does not stop the delete from labeling the originating event after the fact.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 3 and Invariant 5 — the death-signal, even sender-fired, leaks ground-truth labels for real vs cover.

attacks.title: Trial-decrypt 'have/want' Bloom gossip leaks interest and arrival ordering
attacks.mechanism: §15 specifies Bloom 'have/want' gossip to avoid re-sending. A 'want' set reveals which IDs a phone does NOT yet have => arrival-ordering => a sensor can reconstruct the spanning tree of who-saw-what-first, sharpening first-emergence. Worse, if 'want' is ever derived from inbox/trial-decrypt outcomes (e.g., a phone stops re-broadcasting a blob it recognized as its own because inbox policy purged it), the *change in re-broadcast behavior* for a specific ID localizes the recipient — the same class of bug the v0.1 tombstone had, re-entering through the gossip-efficiency layer.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 2 (no targeting metadata) and Invariant 4 — gossip-set deltas reconstruct propagation topology and can localize the recipient.

attacks.title: iOS background throttling makes the originator's emission profile non-uniform vs Android
attacks.mechanism: §12.5 concedes iOS background BLE is throttled. That means an iOS phone's emission cadence is OS-distinguishable from Android's, and a foregrounded (actively-used = likely-originating) iOS phone emits differently from a backgrounded one. An adversary partitions the population by OS-cadence fingerprint and by foreground/background state; 'user just opened the app to send' becomes detectable as a cadence change. The charge-independent uniformity floor (§10) cannot hold uniformly across an OS that structurally forbids it.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 3 — platform-imposed cadence differences break the cross-device uniformity the design assumes.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Receive-before-originate gate ('muddle the first emergence'): never let a real blob be the first thing your radio emits
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: First-emergence/first-spy provenance (attack 1); cover-vs-real separability (attack 5)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Borrow the Dandelion++ insight inverted for flooding. A phone NEVER originates a fresh real ID 'cold.' It maintains a continuous outbound stream that is dominated by RELAYED blobs (others' soup) plus dummies, and it injects a real origination only by *substituting* it into a slot where it would otherwise have re-broadcast an existing blob, after a randomized hold ('muddle delay') drawn from the same distribution as its relay-forwarding jitter. Crucially, before originating, the phone waits until it has recently RECEIVED and re-emitted >= k distinct novel IDs from neighbors in this window, so the venue's first-sighting graph shows your phone forwarding novel IDs constantly — your real one is statistically buried among the novel IDs you were ALREADY relaying. The first-spy estimator now points at whoever genuinely sourced each of the k novel IDs you relayed, not preferentially at you. This is the gossip-protocol natural-DP result (Bellet-Guerraoui-Hendrikx 2019/2020) made deliberate: source-DP improves with relay depth and neighbor count before broadcast.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Directly attacks the single worst attack (first-emergence) using the SAME flooding mechanic, no routing, no targeting. Turns 'cover that propagates like real' into 'real that is timed exactly like the relayed novel-ID stream you already produce.'
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Added origination latency (seconds to minutes in sparse venues — gated by k novel-ID arrivals); needs a minimum neighbor/novelty density to fire, so it gracefully degrades to a UX warning in sparse mode (consistent with §10 density-aware UX). Modest scheduler complexity.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Phone-local Poisson/loop mixing of the OUTBOUND blob queue (Loopix on a handset)
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: First-emergence timing (attack 1); PoW re-mine power tell (attack 4); cover separability (attack 5)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Treat each phone as a one-hop Loopix mix for its own outbound queue. Maintain a single send queue holding {relayed blobs, dummies, your real blobs}. Pop from it at exponential inter-emission times (Poisson process), so the outbound stream's timing carries zero information about WHICH blob is which — a real origination, a relay, and a dummy are emitted from one indistinguishable Poisson source. Add Loopix-style 'loop' blobs (sealed to yourself) that you emit and later recognize on trial-decrypt; their presence in the same queue means even YOUR successful trial-decrypts are not all 'incoming real' — some are your own loops, breaking the inbox-behavior tell (attack 7). This is the most battle-tested phone-feasible mixing primitive (Loopix handles 300 msg/s on a server; a phone's blob rate is orders of magnitude lower, so the Poisson scheduler is nearly free).
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Single mechanism that simultaneously (a) hides origination timing, (b) provides self-test loops that double as cover AND active-attack detection (Loopix's loops detect n-1/flooding attacks), (c) makes 'I just trial-decrypted something mine' non-distinguishable from 'I just received my own loop.'
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Adds mean queueing delay (tunable; seconds). Slightly higher emission volume from loop traffic. Exponential scheduler is trivial CPU/battery.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Receiver-bound cover instead of sealed-to-null cover ('dummies addressed to real contacts')
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Cover-vs-real separability (attack 5); active-delete labeling (attack 6); SDA pair-linkage (attack 2)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Replace 'sealed-to-null' dummies with dummies sealed to ACTUAL contacts in your address book (or to plausible decoy pubkeys you also hold private halves for), following Mallesh-Wright 'receiver-bound cover traffic.' Recipients trial-decrypt them, recognize them as cover (a flag inside the seal), and DISCARD silently — but from the outside, a dummy now has the SAME downstream event profile as a real message: it can be ACKed-by-cover, it can be actively-deleted-by-cover, it triggers the recipient's inbox machinery. The originator periodically fires fake ACKs and fake 'secret' reveals for dummies, so the population of {messages that ever get an ACK / a delete} is no longer 100% real. This is the specific defense the SDA literature prescribes and it directly closes attacks 5 and 6.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Removes the cleanest ground-truth labels (ACK-exists, delete-exists, inbox-reaction-exists) that bootstrap every intersection attack. Makes the real:cover ratio meaningful at the EVENT level, not just the byte level.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Bends Invariant 5 slightly: you are now minting fake delete/'secret' events, which means the structural-wall claim ('only sender+recipient can mint a delete') becomes 'only sender+recipient can mint a delete OR a cover-delete' — still forge-resistant against outsiders, but the sender now deliberately emits decoy deletes. Higher cover volume; address-book leakage risk if decoy-keys are mishandled (mitigate by using your own loop-keys as the 'contacts').

constructiveAlternatives.title: Quantify and PUBLISH the k-anonymity / real:cover budget as a first-class, congestion-adaptive parameter
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: The unquantified-uniformity hole flagged in the assessment; cover realism (open question c)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Define an explicit target: 'a real origination must be hidden among >= K indistinguishable origination-events within the same spatial cell and time window.' Each phone estimates local origination rate from the novel-ID arrival rate it observes (it is ALREADY observing every novel ID), and scales its own dummy origination so that locally observed (real+cover) origination rate per cell stays above the K-floor. In a dense venue, little cover is needed (humans supply the crowd); in a thin area, the floor demands MORE cover or the density-aware UX (§10) refuses/warns. This converts the vague 'match bursty/diurnal' goal into a measurable control loop with a stated guarantee, and resolves the tension (matching human bursts vs constant cover) by making cover *fill the gaps* in the human burst envelope rather than imitating it blindly.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Turns the central unverifiable claim ('uniformity') into a measurable, testable invariant with a number. Lets you state honestly 'anonymity set >= K' or warn when it is not met. Directly answers open question (c).
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Requires a local density/origination estimator and a feedback controller; some battery for extra cover in sparse regimes; a real number you must defend in the threat model (but that is the point).

constructiveAlternatives.title: PHY-fingerprint countermeasures: rotate/duty-cycle radios, randomize TX power, and budget a PHY audit
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: PHY-layer device fingerprinting (attack 3); OS-cadence partition (attack 8)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Accept that perfect PHY-unlinkability is unattainable on COTS hardware (state this honestly), but raise the cost: (1) randomize transmit power and advertising interval per emission within BLE-legal bounds, which degrades CFO/RSSI-based localization and the cadence fingerprint; (2) where a device has multiple radios (BLE + Wi-Fi Aware, deferred in §15), alternate carriers so no single radiometric signature accumulates enough samples; (3) prefer 'send when the crowd is also bursting' (couple to the K-floor controller) so your handset's samples are temporally interleaved with many others, frustrating per-device classifier training that needs clean per-device sample runs; (4) commission a radiometric red-team in the §10 'transport audit' budget — measure your OWN fingerprintability with a USRP and report it as a known limitation with a measured number.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Honest, partial mitigation of an attack the spec currently waves away; converts an unbounded assumed-loss into a measured, cost-raised residual. Couples cleanly to the K-floor controller (alt #4).
constructiveAlternatives.cost: TX-power randomization slightly hurts reliable delivery range; multi-radio alternation is deferred-feature territory; a PHY audit costs money. None of it fully defeats a determined RFFI adversary — be explicit about that.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Relay-equalized PoW: relays also spend (cheap) work, so re-mining is not originator-exclusive
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: PoW re-mine power side channel (attack 4)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Today only originators spend PoW; relays forward free, so only originators ever re-mine (the tell). Equalize the work budget: require each phone to maintain a continuous, low-rate PoW 'heartbeat' (e.g., minting cover blobs and refreshing a rolling pool) at a rate INDEPENDENT of whether it originated anything — driven by the same Poisson queue as emission. The phone always re-mines at a steady background rate to keep its dummy pool full; a real origination consumes from the same pool but does not change the refill schedule. Power/thermal draw is then a function of the (constant) heartbeat, not of origination. Combine with the charge-independent floor (§10) so the heartbeat is battery-state-independent within the floor.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Closes the re-mine side channel by making PoW expenditure a constant background process decoupled from origination, consistent with the existing pre-mine philosophy.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Steady battery cost for continuous low-rate PoW even when idle (this is the price of power-uniformity); must be tuned to the weakest battery phone (defender constraint already acknowledged in §8).

constructiveAlternatives.title: Coarsen the Bloom 'have/want' gossip into epoch-batched, padded set reconciliation
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Gossip-set delta topology leak (attack 7)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Instead of fine-grained per-blob 'want' messages that reveal arrival ordering, reconcile sets only on a coarse epoch boundary (e.g., every T seconds) using a FIXED-SIZE, padded Invertible Bloom Lookup Table (IBLT) / set-reconciliation digest whose size does not depend on how many novel IDs you actually want. Never change re-broadcast behavior for a specific ID based on inbox/trial-decrypt outcome — keep re-broadcasting recognized-as-mine blobs as cover until their TTL (you already trial-decrypt once; just don't STOP relaying it, which would localize you). This removes both the arrival-ordering channel and the recipient-localizing behavior-change.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Keeps gossip-efficiency benefits while removing the ordering/interest side channel; the 'keep relaying your own blob' rule closes the recipient-localization re-entry of the old tombstone bug.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Slightly less efficient reconciliation (padded/epoch-batched), so marginally more redundant transfers; IBLT decode CPU is small at these volumes.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Spatial-cell decoy relays / 'human ferry equalization' to flatten first-emergence triangulation
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: First-emergence triangulation by RSSI multilateration (attack 1)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: First-emergence triangulation needs the originator to be the spatially-nearest source of a novel ID. Weaken the spatial signal: phones rebroadcast novel blobs they just received with randomized short delay AND at randomized power, so the 'who emitted it first and strongest' RSSI gradient is muddied across many nearby phones within the muddle window. In dense venues (the accepted scope), within one muddle window a novel ID is being emitted by dozens of phones at varying power, so RSSI multilateration cannot resolve THE first emitter. This is essentially Loopix loop-cover + power randomization applied to relayed novelty, and it is *only* effective at gathering density — which is exactly the design's scope, turning the density requirement into an anonymity asset rather than only a delivery requirement.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Uses density (already required for delivery) to actively defeat spatial source-localization — aligns the scope assumption with the anonymity guarantee. No routing, no targeting.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Power randomization trades some delivery range; effectiveness craters in sparse mode (be explicit: sparse => first-emergence is detectable, matching §12.2 percolation honesty).

creativeIdeas: Reframe the whole anonymity argument from 'per-blob indistinguishability' to 'origination-event indistinguishability within a spatiotemporal anonymity set of size K.' This is the honest, defensible claim; the per-blob claim is true but does not deliver what users think it does. Make K the headline metric, measured live, surfaced in the density-aware UX ('your message will be hidden among ~37 origination events').

Lean into the gossip natural-differential-privacy result as the formal backbone: under flooding on a well-connected graph, source-DP epsilon improves with (neighbor degree) and (relay depth before broadcast). That gives you a real theorem to cite for why the receive-before-originate gate works, and a knob (k novel-IDs before origination) that maps to an epsilon. This directly answers open question (h) reach-in-sparse: below percolation, degree->1, epsilon->infinity, source is exposed — a clean formal statement of why sparse mode must warn.

Self-loop blobs (Loopix loops sealed to yourself) are a triple-use primitive worth adopting wholesale: cover traffic + active-attack/n-1 detection + breaking the 'successful-trial-decrypt == incoming real message' inbox tell. One mechanism, three open questions dented (c, d, and the trial-decrypt-behavior leak).

Treat PHY-fingerprinting as a STATED, MEASURED limitation rather than an assumed-bounded one. Run your own USRP red-team, publish 'we are X% linkable across MAC rotation at Y meters,' and design the rest of the system to be safe-ish even when device-linkage is assumed = 1.0 (which forces you toward the K-anonymity-of-origination-events framing, because that is the ONLY thing that survives perfect device linkage).

Make cover traffic 'fill the human burst envelope' rather than 'imitate human bursts.' The control loop: observe the crowd's aggregate origination rate; emit cover only to top the local rate up to the K-floor, concentrating your dummies in the troughs of human activity (late night, lulls) where real traffic is thin and SDA is otherwise easiest. This resolves the constant-cover-vs-human-burst contradiction by making cover anti-correlated with real volume.

Consider deliberately-shared origination 'mint windows': clients only release freshly-originated real blobs at globally-synchronized epoch ticks (e.g., aligned to wall-clock minute boundaries), so every real origination in the venue appears in the same batch. This is mix-style batching on the originator side; it converts continuous first-emergence (highly localizable) into batched first-emergence (every novel ID in the venue appears at once => first-spy estimator has a tie among the whole batch). Latency cost is bounded by the epoch length; it is the strongest single anti-first-emergence move and is pure-flooding-compatible.

Audit the active-delete 'secret' reveal as a research-grade label leak: even with random delay it provides ground-truth {this ID was real}. Either (a) emit cover 'secret' reveals for dummies (receiver-bound cover, alt #3), or (b) default active-delete to OFF and document that turning it on measurably degrades long-term anonymity by feeding the adversary labels — quantify how many labeled reals an SDA needs to converge (Mathewson-Danezis give the rate) and state the price.

techniques.name: Dandelion++ (Fanti, Venkatakrishnan et al., SIGMETRICS 2018) and the 'first-spy estimator'
techniques.relevance: The canonical formalization that broadcast/flooding exposes the source to the first adversarial observer, and the stem/muddle remedy. Directly motivates the receive-before-originate gate and the batched mint-window idea. The estimator is the exact threat behind attack 1.

techniques.name: Bellet, Guerraoui, Hendrikx, 'Who Started This Rumor? Quantifying the Natural Differential Privacy of Gossip Protocols' (arXiv 1902.07138; DISC 2020)
techniques.relevance: Gives a formal source-DP guarantee for gossip that improves with neighbor degree and relay/muting parameter — the theoretical backbone for why muddling before origination buys measurable anonymity, and why sparse graphs lose it. Use for the epsilon-vs-density argument.

techniques.name: Danezis, 'Statistical Disclosure Attacks' (2003) and Mathewson-Danezis, 'Practical Traffic Analysis: Extending and Resisting Statistical Disclosure' (PET 2004)
techniques.relevance: The intersection/SDA framework behind attack 2; also gives convergence rates (how many observations to deanonymize) you can cite to quantify the active-delete label-leak cost and the K-floor sizing.

techniques.name: Mallesh & Wright, 'Countering Statistical Disclosure with Receiver-Bound Cover Traffic' (ESORICS 2007)
techniques.relevance: Direct prior art that sender-only / addressed-to-null cover is weak against SDA and receiver-bound cover is the fix — the basis for constructive alternative #3 and the critique in attack 5.

techniques.name: Piotrowska, Hayes, Elahi, Meiser, Danezis, 'The Loopix Anonymity System' (USENIX Security 2017)
techniques.relevance: Poisson mixing + loop cover traffic, both portable to a single phone's outbound queue (alts #2, #8, and the self-loop creative idea). Loop traffic doubles as active-attack detection. Throughput numbers show the scheduler is essentially free at phone blob rates.

techniques.name: BLE RF fingerprinting literature: 'Identifying Bluetooth Low Energy Devices' (ACM SenSys 2021); RFFI-for-BLE works (arXiv 2402.06250, 2510.09940, 2506.09807, 2024-2025)
techniques.relevance: Empirical basis for attack 3 — CFO/I-Q-transient device fingerprints survive MAC and payload rotation at high accuracy with commodity SDR. Justifies treating device-linkage as ~1.0 and reframing around origination-event anonymity. (Exact accuracy numbers vary by paper/SNR — treat the 84-99% range as indicative, not a single cited figure.)

techniques.name: Invertible Bloom Lookup Tables (IBLT) / set reconciliation (Eppstein-Goodrich; Graphene for Bitcoin)
techniques.relevance: Fixed-size, padded epoch reconciliation to remove the arrival-ordering / 'want'-set side channel in attack 7 (alt #7) while keeping gossip efficiency.

techniques.name: Bluetooth Mesh Private Beacons / address-carryover defenses (already in spec §14)
techniques.relevance: Correct baseline for ID-free advertisements; note it only stops cheap passive sniffers, NOT PHY fingerprinting — the spec already half-acknowledges this and should be explicit that it is necessary-not-sufficient.

analyses.dimension: DELETION — sender-owned active delete + private sealed ack (meldingx v0.2 §7), and "ephemeral + optionally erasable" generally
analyses.assessment: The v0.2 deletion model is a genuine improvement over v0.1: moving the emitter from recipient to sender removes the most obvious recipient-localization leak, and making the ack an ordinary sealed blob means the recipient truly emits nothing special. The default (no active delete, local-purge + signed absolute TTL) is the strong, honest core and I would defend it hard — it leaks nothing because no delete-shaped event ever exists. The trouble is concentrated entirely in the OPTIONAL active-delete path, and §12.3 already half-admits it ("the lone metadata handle"). My deeper finding is that the residual leak is worse than the spec frames it, in three compounding ways the doc does not separate. (1) The delete is not just "a phone deleted its own message" to a wide adversary — because `message-ID = hash(secret)` and the broadcast reveals `secret`, ANY node that ever carried that blob can now verify, retroactively and offline, that this specific blob was a REAL message, not cover. That collapses the cover-traffic k-anonymity set for that blob to zero after the fact, which is exactly the property §1/§10 are built to protect. (2) The delete event is an origination-shaped signal at the sender, and a persistent or even episodic intersection/statistical-disclosure adversary (Danezis et al.) can use the set of "phones that emitted a verified-real reveal" to build a sender-side fingerprint over many messages — uniformity protects you per-message, but the delete opt-in re-introduces a per-message ground-truth label that intersection attacks feast on. (3) The "structural wall" the spec names (you cannot hide a real delete in decoy deletes because only two parties can mint one) is real and, I think, fatal to ever making active-delete fully uniform — so the right move is NOT to chase decoy deletes but to change what a delete IS so that it carries no verifiable real/cover label and no fresh emission. Crucially, active delete is also only ADVISORY: a hostile relay simply ignores `secret`, and §2/§12.4 concede durable deletion against retained ciphertext is out of scope — so the feature spends real anonymity budget to buy a guarantee it cannot actually deliver against the adversary who matters most. That asymmetry (costs anonymity, delivers only against honest nodes) is the thing to fix.

attacks.title: Reveal of `secret` retroactively labels a specific blob as REAL, destroying its cover-anonymity
attacks.mechanism: message-ID = hash(secret). The active delete broadcasts `secret` in clear. Any node that ever held that blob (or logged its ID) can compute hash(secret) and confirm 'blob X was a genuine message, not a sealed-to-null dummy.' Cover traffic's whole job (§1, §10) is that real and dummy are indistinguishable forever. The delete provides an after-the-fact oracle that flips exactly one blob from 'unknown' to 'confirmed real.' A passive recorder who kept blob IDs + RF timestamps can then go back to its logs, find which phone FIRST emitted that now-confirmed-real ID, and learn 'that phone originated a real message at that time.' The random delay on the delete protects the DELETE timing, not the original ORIGINATION timing already in the logs.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 3 (anonymity via uniformity: real indistinguishable from cover) and Invariant 4 (sealed-sender: origination not attributable).

attacks.title: Sender-fired delete is an intersection-attack ground-truth label that deanonymizes senders over time
attacks.mechanism: Per-message uniformity does not protect against repeated observation. Each active-delete event marks (phone P, time T) as 'P originated a real message.' A persistent or even part-time wide-coverage adversary (the §3 'persistent, wide-coverage' actor) collects these labels and runs a classic statistical-disclosure / intersection attack: the set of phones present-and-emitting whenever a given recipient's contact is active narrows toward the true sender. Because active-delete is opt-in per message, a security-conscious user who turns it on for their MOST sensitive messages is precisely labeling their most sensitive originations — an availability-vs-anonymity inversion. §12.3 acknowledges a residual; it understates that the residual is a *labeled training set* for disclosure attacks, not just a one-off.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 4 (sender hidden from everyone but recipient) and Invariant 3 (uniformity) under multi-observation.

attacks.title: Ack injection still leaks the recipient via timing + presence correlation, despite being sealed
attacks.mechanism: The ack is content-indistinguishable junk, yes — but it is a NEW first-emission that must enter the soup from the recipient's location shortly after the recipient reads. To a co-located/SDR adversary near the recipient (§3 SDR actor links packets to a handset), the recipient's read produces a fresh-ID emission within some window of receiving the original. More damaging: the adversary who already (via Attack 1) knows blob X is a real message addressed to *someone* can watch which phones in range emit a fresh blob shortly after blob X passes through their vicinity — the recipient's ack is one of those. Sealing hides ack CONTENT, not the fact that a phone minted a fresh ID at a suggestive time/place. The 'recipient emits nothing special, ever' claim is true only if ack emission is timing-decoupled from read AND statistically buried in that phone's normal first-emission cover rate — which the spec asserts but does not budget.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 4 (recipient hidden) — partial; Invariant 3 (uniformity of emissions) at the timing layer.

attacks.title: Active delete is advisory-only: hostile relay non-compliance makes the guarantee hollow while the leak is real
attacks.mechanism: Nothing forces a node to honor `secret`. A logging adversary keeps the ciphertext AND now also learns `secret` (so can mark the blob real — Attack 1) while simply not deleting. The honest network deletes; the adversary retains. So active delete provides erasure ONLY against parties who were going to let the message die at TTL anyway, and provides a fresh deanonymization signal to exactly the party who won't delete. The feature's cost (anonymity) lands on the honest case; its benefit (early erasure) does not land on the adversarial case. §12.4 concedes durability is out of scope, but the spec still presents active-delete as a positive feature without netting cost against the near-zero benefit vs the threat actor it's implicitly sold against ('erase worldwide now').
attacks.breaks: Invariant 5 (self-destruct as a *guarantee*) — it is downgraded to a hint; and net-negative on Invariant 3/4.

attacks.title: `secret`-grinding / shadow-ID and pre-image replay around the seen-record window
attacks.mechanism: Two sub-attacks. (a) Shadow-ID: an attacker who learns a victim's message-ID (it is on the wire, by design) cannot find `secret` (pre-image resistance) but CAN flood many blobs whose IDs collide in the truncated index/Bloom seen-record (if any truncation is used anywhere in indexing) to cause false-positive drops of the victim's real blob — a targeted suppression. The spec wisely keeps the FULL 256-bit ID security-critical, which defends the crypto, but any *operational* truncation (Bloom buckets, BLE advertisement prefixes used for 'have/want' gossip in §15) reintroduces a collision-grinding surface against a *named* message. (b) Reveal-replay: once `secret` is public, an attacker re-injects the delete repeatedly, or injects `secret` for a blob whose 30-day seen-record entry is about to expire vs whose creation predates it, probing nodes' purge behavior to map who still holds what. Single-purge-then-suppress (§7) mostly handles (b) but depends on the seen-record never aging out a still-relevant ID under clock skew/partition.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 5 (orderly death) and availability of the target message (Invariant 1 full-function offline).

attacks.title: Coerced / rubber-hose deletion has no deniability and the ack gives the coercer a confirmation oracle
attacks.mechanism: A coercer holding the sender can force broadcast of `secret`, proving (via hash) that a specific captured blob was the sender's real message — converting a seized phone into proof-of-authorship for any blob the coercer points at, retroactively. Worse, because delivery confirmation is now PRIVATE TO THE SENDER (the sealed ack, the spec calls this a 'bonus'), a coercer who seizes the sender also seizes the read-receipts — learning that the recipient READ it and roughly when the ack arrived. The duress wipe (§6) rotates identity but does not retroactively unprove an already-revealed `secret`. The model has strong forge-resistance (good) but zero coercion-deniability on the authorship direction.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 4 (identity inside the seal stays private) under §3 seizure actor; weakens the 'leave no durable trace' endgoal.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Decouple the delete token from `secret`: delete via an independent, single-use 'erase-tag' that proves authority WITHOUT revealing the message-ID pre-image
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Attack 1 (reveal labels blob as real) and Attack 6 (coercion proof-of-authorship).
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Today delete == publish `secret` where hash(secret)==message-ID, so the reveal IS a proof the blob is real. Instead, at send time derive TWO independent secrets from the sealed inner payload: keep message-ID = hash(secret_id) as now, but make the active-delete token a SEPARATE value erase-tag = hash(secret_del), and put a commitment to hash(erase-tag) (call it delete-commit) INSIDE the seal, not in the public immutable core. To delete, the sender broadcasts erase-tag. A node honors it by: trial-opening blobs it can read? No — relays can't read. So the delete blob carries the public message-ID it targets PLUS erase-tag; an honest relay checks that this is a well-formed delete for an ID it holds and purges its copy. Critically, erase-tag is NOT the pre-image of the public message-ID, so revealing it does NOT let a logger prove the original blob was real vs cover — it only proves 'someone authorized to delete ID Y did so,' and cover/dummy blobs ALSO get periodic fake erase-tags emitted (you CAN mint decoy deletes for your own dummies, breaking the 'structural wall' for the dummy population). The real/cover label is gone because the reveal no longer opens the message-ID commitment.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Removes the retroactive real-vs-cover oracle (the core leak). Lets you emit decoy deletes for your own cover blobs, so a delete event no longer implies a real message existed. Coercer can no longer use the reveal to prove authorship of the original content (erase-tag commits to nothing readable).
constructiveAlternatives.cost: ~32 extra bytes inside the seal (a commitment), negligible. Slightly more complex delete validation. Decoy deletes add a modest emission/PoW budget. Does not fix hostile-relay non-compliance (Attack 4) by itself — pair with Alt 4.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Crypto-shred by default: make every message forward-secure-to-deletion so 'delete' is a LOCAL key-puncture, never a broadcast
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Attack 1, 2, 4 (any leak that comes from a delete being ON THE WIRE) and the whole 'delete spends anonymity' problem.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Lean all the way into the strong default. Use the one-time-prekey/puncturable-encryption machinery (§4 already has X3DH prekeys; Green & Miers 'Forward Secure Asynchronous Messaging from Puncturable Encryption', CCS 2015) so that READING a message punctures the recipient's key for that message: after read, the recipient can no longer decrypt that ciphertext even if it is re-fed to them, and the prekey private half is destroyed (§4 already says 'recipient deletes its private half on read'). Generalize this: the message's payload key is itself derived so that local purge + key-puncture = the ciphertext is permanently undecryptable BY ANYONE WHO DID NOT ALREADY OPEN IT. Now 'self-destruct' for the recipient and for any honest holder is achieved with ZERO wire signal — the blob still dies at TTL on relays, and is crypto-shredded the instant the recipient reads. Active 'erase from others now' is reframed honestly: it cannot beat a retained-ciphertext adversary anyway (§12.4), so DON'T sell it; sell 'unreadable to the recipient and to future key-seizure the moment they read, plus TTL death everywhere else.' This is the Vanish lesson inverted: Vanish trusted DHT churn to lose key shares and was beaten by Sybil DHT crawling (Wolchok et al., NDSS 2010) — so do NOT rely on the network to forget; rely on local puncture you control.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Eliminates the on-wire delete signal entirely for the common case (read-then-shred), so Attacks 1/2/3/4 lose their substrate. Strengthens forward secrecy against §3 seizure. Honest about what deletion can deliver.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Puncturable encryption is heavier than plain X25519 (key grows with #punctures, or needs the compact/Bloom-filter-encryption variants — Green-Miers use a Bloom-filter-based scheme with bounded false-deletion). CPU/storage budget on weak phones (ties to open problem (e) trial-decrypt cost). Does not give 'erase from others NOW' — but the spec already concedes that's unachievable against the real adversary.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Batch + jitter the delete into the existing first-emission cover stream so it is one emission among many, with mandatory decoy-delete floor
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Attack 2 (intersection labeling) and Attack 3 (ack/delete timing).
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: The spec's random delay protects one event; replace it with a STRUCTURAL cover floor specifically for deletes. Every phone, all the time, emits a low baseline rate of decoy-delete blobs (for its OWN cover blobs — possible once delete is decoupled from message-ID, Alt 1; OR for expired IDs it legitimately knows). Real deletes are released only inside the next scheduled decoy-delete slot (Poisson/diurnal-matched, per §10), so a real delete is statistically buried in a constant stream of deletes that mostly correspond to nothing real. This converts the 'structural wall' (can't decoy a REAL message's delete) into 'don't need to — the DELETE-EVENT population is full of decoys even if each specific real-message delete is unique.' Combine with Alt 1 so the decoys are indistinguishable from real deletes (same shape, no real/cover label on reveal).
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Turns the delete from a rare labeled event (great for intersection attacks) into a high-rate noisy channel where the per-message label is drowned. Directly attacks the statistical-disclosure substrate.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Continuous delete-cover costs battery/airtime (adds to §10 cover budget). Only meaningful if Alt 1 lands (otherwise decoys are distinguishable from real deletes by the hash check). Reduces but does not zero the residual against a truly persistent global recorder (§3 out-of-model anyway).

constructiveAlternatives.title: Make active-delete a recipient-side LOCAL crypto-shred triggered by a TTL-bucketed epoch key, no per-message wire event at all
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Attack 3 (ack), Attack 1/2 (any wire delete), and the 'recipient beacon' ban in the focus prompt.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Bind each message to a coarse EPOCH (e.g. 6-hour buckets within the ≤7d TTL). The sealed payload is encrypted under an epoch sub-key the recipient derives and stores. 'Disappear-after-read' and 'sender wants it gone' both reduce to: the recipient destroys the relevant epoch sub-key locally (puncture). No ack, no broadcast, no fresh emission — pure local action, the strongest possible version of 'recipient emits nothing special.' The sender's 'I want it deleted' is communicated as part of the ORIGINAL message (a flag inside the seal: 'shred on read' / 'shred at epoch boundary'), so there is no second message ever. This abandons 'erase others' copies now' (kept by TTL only) but the spec already concedes that's the unachievable part. It fully preserves the no-recipient-beacon requirement and removes the ack channel that Attack 3 exploits.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Zero wire signal for deletion in the dominant case. No ack to time-correlate. Honors the explicit 'no recipient beacon' constraint perfectly. Cheap on phone (key destruction).
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Bends Invariant 5's 'optional sender-owned ACTIVE delete that propagates': there is no longer any way to push an early erase to RELAYS before TTL — relays still only die at TTL. So 'sender can erase from the world early' is lost; you keep 'sender can pre-decide it shreds on read.' If the product genuinely needs sender-fired global early-erase, this alone is insufficient (use Alt 1 for that path).

constructiveAlternatives.title: If sender-fired global delete is kept, make compliance VERIFIABLE-but-cheap and gossip it via have/want so non-compliance is at least costly, not free
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Attack 4 (hostile relay non-compliance) and Attack 5b (reveal-replay).
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Today a relay can silently ignore `secret`/erase-tag. Add the deleted ID to the SAME 30-day Bloom seen-record that already exists, so an honest node that processed a valid delete will (a) purge and (b) refuse to re-accept that ID — meaning a non-compliant retainer cannot RE-SEED the blob into the honest mesh (it becomes an island). It can keep its private copy (unfixable, §12.4) but it can no longer use the mesh to keep the message alive or to probe others. Pair with single-purge-then-suppress (already in §7) so replayed deletes are idempotent. Optionally, deletes carry a tiny PoW (already proposed in §7) AND a short own-TTL ≤ message-TTL so the delete-blob itself dies cleanly. This makes the honest mesh converge to 'gone' fast and confines retained copies to non-participating islands.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Best achievable against retained ciphertext within the model: you cannot force deletion, but you remove the attacker's ability to use the mesh against you (re-seeding, resurrection, probing). Tightens the gap between honest-network erasure and TTL.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Slight growth of seen-record write rate (already budgeted, §6). Does not and cannot delete the adversary's private copy (honest, in-scope limitation). Must guard against using deletes themselves as a flooding vector — bind to a held ID + PoW.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Coercion-deniable receipts: keep delivery confirmation but make it forgeable-by-the-recipient (plausible-deniability ack)
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Attack 6 (coercer learns the message was read; private receipt becomes a liability under seizure).
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: The 'bonus' that delivery confirmation is private to the sender becomes a coercion liability: seize the sender, learn the reads. Borrow the OTR/Signal deniability idea: make the ack a MAC under a key both parties can compute (the X3DH-derived shared secret), so the sender can verify it BUT could equally have forged it himself. Then a seized sender's collection of 'acks' proves nothing to a coercer (the sender could have fabricated them). Optionally let the ack be a constant-shape blob whose presence/absence the sender cannot prove to a third party. This keeps the private-receipt feature for the honest sender while denying its evidentiary value under coercion.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Removes the seizure downside of private receipts; aligns with the project's leave-no-durable-trace endgoal and the existing duress philosophy (§6).
