constructiveAlternatives.cost: Sender loses cryptographic NON-repudiation of receipt (acceptable for this product — it's a feature for deniability). Small protocol change to the ack construction.

creativeIdeas: Reframe the product promise around what is actually deliverable: 'ephemeral by construction' (TTL death everywhere + crypto-shred on read) rather than 'erase from the world on command.' The honest version is MORE trustworthy and removes the single biggest metadata handle. Make active global-delete an expert-only, clearly-labeled 'this emits a detectable signal' toggle, mirroring the existing density-aware UX warnings (§10).

Two-class TTL UX instead of active delete for the common case: a prominent 'whisper' mode (TTL = 1-10 min, 'only if you're near me') and a 'note' mode (hours). Most of the felt need for 'delete it now' is really 'I want it short-lived' — solve it with a short signed TTL (zero wire signal) instead of a delete event. The spec already lists 1-min TTL as a feature; elevate it to a first-class deletion-substitute.

Self-shredding-on-read via puncturable prekeys as the DEFAULT, so 'disappear-after-read' is cryptographic, not just a local file delete — protecting against a phone seized AFTER reading (the §3 seizure actor) without any new wire event.

Decoy-delete population: once delete is decoupled from message-ID (Alt 1), have every phone routinely emit deletes for its OWN cover blobs. This is the only way to populate a decoy-delete crowd given the structural wall, and it makes 'a phone deleted something' a constant, content-free event like first-emission cover.

Epoch-keyed forward erasure (Alt 4) as a unifying primitive: bucket TTL into epochs, key payloads per-epoch, and a single epoch-key puncture shreds whole cohorts at once — cheap, local, no wire signal, and it gives a clean implementation of disappear-after-read AND coarse sender-intent shredding.

Borrow certified-deletion framing carefully: classical certified deletion (Broadbent-Islam is quantum) is not available, but the *spirit* — a verifiable claim 'this is now unrecoverable' — can be approximated locally via attested key-puncture logs the user can show themselves, supporting the duress/panic story without any network proof that could leak.

Treat the ack as part of normal cover, not a special message: only ever emit acks inside the phone's existing first-emission cover slots (Poisson, diurnal-matched), so the recipient's 'read' never produces an off-schedule emission. If there's no scheduled slot soon, the ack waits — receipts are best-effort by design, which is fine for this product.

techniques.name: Green & Miers, 'Forward Secure Asynchronous Messaging from Puncturable Encryption' (IEEE S&P 2015) — Bloom-filter puncturable encryption
techniques.relevance: Directly enables read-then-shred and per-message forward secrecy (Alt 2, Alt 4) so deletion becomes a LOCAL key-puncture with no wire signal. Their Bloom-filter construction bounds key growth and has a tunable false-deletion rate — relevant to weak-phone cost (open problem (e)). PDF at cs.umd.edu/~imiers/pdf/forwardsec.pdf.

techniques.name: Wolchok et al., 'Defeating Vanish with Low-Cost Sybil Attacks Against Large DHTs' (NDSS 2010)
techniques.relevance: Cautionary prior art: Vanish relied on network churn to FORGET key shares; Sybil DHT crawling recovered >99% of 'expired' data. Lesson for meldingx: never rely on relays/network to forget (they are the adversary, §3) — base deletion on local puncture you control, not on hostile nodes honoring TTL/delete. jhalderm.com/pub/papers/unvanish-ndss10-web.pdf.

techniques.name: Danezis & Serjantov, statistical disclosure / intersection attacks (PET 2003-2004); 'The Reverse Statistical Disclosure Attack'
techniques.relevance: Formalizes exactly Attack 2: per-message uniformity does not stop an adversary who collects repeated ground-truth labels. The opt-in active-delete supplies those labels. Use to justify the decoy-delete floor (Alt 3) and to quantify open problem (d) 'active-delete residual.'

techniques.name: Crypto-shredding / crypto-erase (well-established practice)
techniques.relevance: The honest framing of deletion here: render data unrecoverable by destroying keys, not by trusting holders to delete bytes. Underpins Alt 2/Alt 4 and the 'reframe the promise' idea. Aligns with what §12.4 already concedes.

techniques.name: OTR / Signal deniability (Borisov-Goldberg-Brewer 2004; Signal X3DH deniability properties)
techniques.relevance: MAC-under-shared-key acks the sender could have forged (Alt 6) remove the coercion/evidentiary value of the now-private receipt (Attack 6) while keeping the receipt useful to an honest sender.

techniques.name: Martiny et al., 'Improving Signal's Sealed Sender' (NDSS 2021) — delivery-receipt deanonymization
techniques.relevance: Already cited in the spec; reinforces that ANY receipt/delete that recurs is an intersection vector. Validates pushing receipts/deletes into cover and minimizing their count (Alt 3, creative idea 7).

techniques.name: Shibboleth/RLN nullifiers and blind-signed n-times tokens (referenced in §8/§13 for rate-limiting)
techniques.relevance: Same primitive family could mint single-use 'delete authority' tokens decoupled from message-ID (supports Alt 1) so a delete proves authorization without revealing the message pre-image. Mark as speculative for the delete use-case — I have not seen it applied to deletes specifically.

techniques.name: Adaptive padding (Shmatikov & Wang, ESORICS 2006) and constant-rate cover with Poisson cover (Loopix, Piotrowska et al., USENIX Sec 2017)
techniques.relevance: Concrete cover-traffic shapes for burying the ack/delete in human-like first-emission traffic (Alt 3, creative idea 7); Loopix's Poisson mixing is the cleanest model for 'emit only in scheduled slots' so reads/deletes cause no off-schedule tell.

analyses.dimension: Deliverability — routing-free reach and the sparse/percolation cliff, without adding routing or requiring internet
analyses.assessment: The spec is unusually honest here: §11 and §12.2 already concede that pure flooding caps scale and that below the percolation threshold offline delivery goes to ~0. So the question isn't "is deliverability a problem" — they know it is — but "how much can you claw back inside the soup invariants?" My read: the dense-gathering case (§11) is genuinely sound — when the whole live soup fits in everyone's buffer, undirected flooding is essentially optimal for delivery and the design works. The real soft spots are three, in order of severity. (1) The percolation CLIFF (§12.2) is a true binary failure, not a graceful degradation: continuum-percolation theory says connectivity has a sharp phase transition at a critical mean node degree (~4.5 neighbors in the 2D unit-disk Gilbert model). One notch below it and the giant component shatters into islands that NEVER exchange a blob, no matter how long the TTL — flooding cannot cross a gap it cannot bridge. This is the dimension's existential risk and the spec's mitigation ("bridges or human ferries") quietly leans on either the internet (violating invariant 1) or unmodeled human behavior. (2) Buffer-eviction-driven loss at the gathering/metropolis boundary (the 2M-blob "yellow" row): once buffers are full, a target blob is competing for a slot against the entire soup, and with attacker-uncorrelated random eviction (correctly chosen for §8) your friend's specific blob is dropped with the same probability as junk — deliverability and abuse-resistance are in direct tension. (3) Reconciliation efficiency: "Bloom have/want gossip" (§15.3) is workable but is the wrong primitive at 1–2M blobs — naive Bloom digests waste bandwidth re-announcing the 99.9% of the soup two neighbors already share, and bandwidth is the binding constraint on a BLE link, which throttles how fast a newly-arrived blob actually saturates a crowd. None of these are fatal to the soul, and all three have soup-preserving fixes. Where I'd push hardest: the cliff needs an explicit, in-scope answer (predictable-mobility ferries + opportunistic Wi-Fi-Aware longer hops) rather than a hand-wave to bridges, and the reconciliation layer should be upgraded to rateless IBLT, which is a pure efficiency win with zero invariant cost.

attacks.title: Percolation cliff is a hard binary, and the spec's fallback violates invariant 1
attacks.mechanism: Continuum percolation has a sharp threshold: below a critical mean degree (~4.5 reachable neighbors in the 2D Gilbert disk model), the network fragments into finite islands with probability ~1; above it, a giant component appears almost surely. Flooding does not 'try harder' across a gap — if two clusters never have a node-pair within BLE range during the TTL, ZERO blobs cross, regardless of buffer size or TTL. The spec's stated mitigation for the sparse/blackout case (ranked the #1 goal) is 'bridges or human ferries carry the distance.' Bridges require internet (violates invariant 1 for the exact regime that most needs the offline guarantee), and 'human ferries' are asserted, not engineered. So the design's top-ranked goal (works in a total blackout) is least supported precisely in the rural/sparse blackout it names as hardest.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 1 (full function with no internet) in the sparse regime; the spec's own ranked-#1 goal.

attacks.title: Eviction fairness weaponizes against deliverability at the buffer boundary
attacks.mechanism: §8 correctly forbids least-PoW eviction and mandates attacker-uncorrelated (randomized) eviction to stop an attacker steering the buffer. But random eviction treats your friend's one real blob identically to a flooder's junk. At the 2M-blob 'yellow' boundary where buffers run ~full, a resourced flooder (accepted as undefeated by PoW, §8/threat table) can inflate the live-blob count so that the per-blob survival probability per eviction cycle drops below what the TTL needs for the target to random-walk to the recipient. The attacker doesn't need to target the victim's blob (can't — it's uniform); they just raise the denominator. Deliverability craters as a SIDE EFFECT of generic flooding, and the anti-abuse design has no per-source lever to stop it (anonymous rate-limiting is open, §13.1). So 'delivery works at gathering scale' holds only in the absence of a flooder — which the threat model explicitly says you cannot exclude.
attacks.breaks: Delivery-works-at-gathering-scale claim (§11); interacts with the unsolved anonymous-rate-limiting open problem (§13.1).

attacks.title: Bloom have/want gossip re-announces the shared 99.9% — bandwidth starves blob saturation
attacks.mechanism: At 1–2M blobs, two neighbors typically already share almost the entire soup; the interesting delta is a handful of new blobs. A plain Bloom 'have' digest scales with the SET SIZE, not the DIFFERENCE, so neighbors spend most of a short BLE contact re-summarizing what both already hold. BLE throughput is the binding constraint (a contact may last seconds in a moving crowd). The result: a freshly-injected real blob propagates slowly through the crowd because contacts are consumed by redundant reconciliation, lowering delivery probability within the TTL — especially for short TTLs (the '1 min = next to me' feature is the most bandwidth-sensitive).
attacks.breaks: Efficiency assumption behind 'your friend's blob survives in everyone's buffer → delivery works' (§11); degrades the short-TTL feature (§6 Knob 1).

attacks.title: Trial-decrypt + reconciliation cost couples to the SAME contact budget as spreading
attacks.mechanism: Open problem §13.5 (trial-decrypt cost at volume) is not just a CPU concern — it competes for the same scarce per-contact window as gossip. With one-time prekeys, each newly-seen blob may need a trial-decrypt against multiple candidate prekeys; if a node receives a burst of new blobs in a brief contact, it must choose between (a) finishing decrypt work and (b) re-broadcasting onward. Either way the effective spreading rate per contact drops. The spec treats decrypt cost and deliverability as separate open questions; they share a budget and should be analyzed jointly.
attacks.breaks: Implicit assumption that carry-and-rebroadcast is ~free per contact (§1); couples §13.5 and the deliverability story.

attacks.title: iOS background throttling silently lowers the effective node degree below the cliff
attacks.mechanism: §12.5 admits iOS background BLE is throttled. The percolation argument depends on COUNT of reachable, actively-relaying neighbors. If a large fraction of phones in a crowd are iOS in background and only weakly relaying, the EFFECTIVE mean degree for sustained relaying can sit below the critical threshold even when the raw device density looks comfortably above it. The crowd looks dense but behaves sparse. A protest with 60% backgrounded iPhones could be on the wrong side of the cliff while appearing to be a 50k-person 'gathering-scale' success case.
attacks.breaks: The mapping from raw density (§11 table) to actual percolation/connectivity; invariant 1 robustness on mixed-OS crowds.

attacks.title: Short-TTL messages have catastrophically worse reach than the spec implies
attacks.mechanism: Reach in flooding is a race between the blob's spreading front and its TTL clock. The spec frames short TTL purely as a privacy/intimacy feature ('1 min = only if you're right next to me'). But because there is no routing, a 1-minute blob can only reach who you literally flood-saturate in 60 seconds — which in a sparse or even moderately-dense moving crowd may be a handful of phones, not necessarily including a recipient 3 hops away. The UX frames short TTL as a choice; it's also a sharp, non-obvious deliverability penalty users won't anticipate.
attacks.breaks: User mental model of Knob 1 (§6); not a security break but an honesty/UX gap that affects perceived deliverability.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Replace Bloom have/want with rateless IBLT set reconciliation (drop-in efficiency win)
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Attack 3 (Bloom re-announces shared soup); indirectly helps Attacks 2 and 4 by freeing contact bandwidth for actual spreading.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Each node treats its set of held message-IDs as a set. On contact, instead of exchanging a full Bloom digest (size ∝ total soup), the two stream rateless-IBLT coded symbols (Practical Rateless Set Reconciliation, Yang et al., SIGCOMM 2024 — open-source Go 'riblt'). The receiver consumes the symbol stream only until it can peel out the SYMMETRIC DIFFERENCE; cost scales with the difference d, not the set size, with ~1.35x communication overhead and O(log d) work per element. Two neighbors who already share 1.99M of 2M blobs reconcile by exchanging on the order of the ~thousands of differing IDs, then transfer only those blobs. It's rateless, so it adapts to however long the contact lasts (perfect for variable BLE windows): a 2-second brush still makes progress; a long sit reconciles fully. Operates purely on opaque message-IDs — no routing, no targeting, no destination metadata.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: 2–2000x lower reconciliation compute and near-optimal bandwidth vs Bloom; converts most of a BLE contact from 'restating the shared soup' into 'actually moving new blobs,' directly raising the saturation speed of a fresh real blob and thus delivery-within-TTL. Pure efficiency; the wire still carries only uniform opaque blobs + content-agnostic coded symbols over IDs.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: More code complexity than a Bloom filter; the symbol stream over IDs is new wire content that must itself be made non-fingerprintable (fixed framing, no stable tokens) to satisfy invariant 4 at the transport layer. Slightly higher constant CPU than a single Bloom check, but far lower at volume.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Opportunistic-mobility ferrying as an ENGINEERED, blind feature (cross the cliff with people, not internet)
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Attack 1 (percolation cliff in sparse/blackout) — the existential one — without bridges and without routing.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Lean INTO the 'message in a bottle' soul: the carrier IS the transport. Make ferrying first-class by maximizing how much soup a moving person carries between disconnected islands, while keeping it totally blind. Concretely: (a) Generous relay-retention default already in §6 Knob 2 is the lever — a phone that walks from island A to island B and dumps its (size-capped) soup on arrival is a ferry, with zero awareness of which blob is for whom. (b) Add 'island-edge detection' purely from local signal: when a node has had no new blobs from any neighbor for T minutes (it's saturated its current island), it should PRESERVE its current soup more aggressively (resist eviction, keep emitting) so it carries maximum payload when it next encounters fresh peers — a mobility-aware retention policy that needs NO position, NO destination, NO routing, just 'am I in a stale pocket.' (c) UX nudge ('you may be carrying messages out of range — staying connected helps everyone') turns voluntary human movement into deterministic ferrying. This is exactly how DTNs achieve delivery in intermittently-connected sparse networks (epidemic/spray-and-wait literature) but stripped of the per-message routing those schemes use — we keep undirected flooding and let MOBILITY, not routing, do the bridging.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Turns the #1-goal weakness (sparse/blackout) from 'delivery → 0, hope someone has internet' into 'delivery delayed but nonzero via ordinary human movement,' the original metaphor. No routing, no targeting, no internet. Carriers stay blind. Stretches reach across percolation gaps that pure same-instant flooding can never cross.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Latency: ferry delivery is bounded by human movement (minutes-to-hours), useless for short-TTL messages. The mobility-aware retention adds a local heuristic and battery cost (carrying more soup longer). Doesn't help if NOBODY moves between islands (true isolation). Bends nothing, but is probabilistic and slow.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Opportunistic longer-range hops (Wi-Fi Aware / NAN, and optional LoRa companion) to push the EFFECTIVE degree above the cliff
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Attacks 1 and 5 (cliff; iOS-throttled effective degree) — physically raise neighbor count without changing the soup model.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: The percolation threshold is about reachable-neighbor COUNT, which is a function of RANGE. BLE is ~10m; the cliff is brutal because each node sees few peers. The spec already defers Wi-Fi Aware (§15). Promote it: run the IDENTICAL uniform-soup protocol over Wi-Fi Aware (NAN) publish/subscribe (range ~100m+, much higher throughput) as an additional, indistinguishable transport. Each ~10x range increase multiplies the neighbor disk area ~100x, moving a crowd that's below the BLE percolation threshold comfortably above it. Same blobs, same trial-decrypt, same cover traffic — just a fatter pipe and longer reach. For true rural blackout, an OPTIONAL LoRa companion (the Bitchat/Meshtastic pattern, km-range) carries the same opaque soup as a long-haul ferry between islands. Crucially LoRa/Wi-Fi-Aware carry the SAME fixed-size sealed blobs with NO routing — they are just longer hops in the same flood.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Directly attacks the cliff via physics (range→degree) rather than protocol cleverness. Wi-Fi Aware also dodges the iOS-background-BLE throttle problem (Attack 5) on supporting devices. LoRa gives a no-internet long-haul option for the blackout-rural case the spec admits is hardest. All transports remain a uniform blind flood.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Wi-Fi Aware has its own MAC/fingerprinting surface that must be hardened to the §10 standard (new transport audit — the spec already budgets one). LoRa duty-cycle regulations and low bandwidth severely limit blob volume (only viable for very low-rate, long-TTL bridging) and a LoRa radio is extra hardware. Wi-Fi Aware support is uneven across devices. Battery cost higher than BLE.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Bloom-rendezvous 'interest beacons' that reveal demand WITHOUT revealing interest (priority signal, not routing)
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Attacks 2 and 3 — give the swarm a way to preferentially retain/spread blobs that someone nearby actually wants, without per-message targeting and without leaking who wants what.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: The hard constraint is invariant 2/4: no per-message targeting, no destination metadata. So DON'T target. Instead, let each node periodically emit an aggregate, padded, k-anonymous Bloom filter of message-IDs it is INTERESTED in receiving — but blinded so it leaks neither the exact IDs nor the count. Concretely: a node's 'want' beacon is a fixed-size Bloom filter seeded with (i) IDs it genuinely awaits PLUS (ii) a calibrated number of random decoy IDs, so the filter's fill rate is constant and reveals only 'this node wants ~some blobs,' never which or how many (cover-traffic logic applied to interest). A neighbor holding a blob whose ID hits ANYONE's want-beacon nudges that blob UP its retention priority and re-broadcast frequency — a soft, statistical pull, not a routed push. Because every node's beacon is padded to the same fill and includes decoys, an observer cannot tell a real want from a decoy (uniformity, invariant 3), and the holder can't tell WHO wants it (it's an aggregate hit). This is 'interest without revealing interest' via deliberately-leaky-but-decoyed Bloom rendezvous — a known PSI-lite/private-membership pattern, here tuned for k-anonymity rather than cryptographic PSI to stay cheap on a phone.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Gives deliverability a steering signal that survives a flooder (a junk blob hits no want-beacon, so it gets DE-prioritized — a soft anti-abuse lever that needs no identity, partially addressing Attack 2's 'random eviction can't protect the real blob'). Raises the survival probability of wanted blobs near recipients without any path or destination on the wire.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: BENDS INVARIANT 4 slightly: a want-beacon is technically a new kind of metadata on the wire (a fuzzy, decoyed hint of demand). The decoy padding must be tuned carefully or the k-anonymity set collapses; a persistent wide adversary doing statistical disclosure over many beacons (explicitly out-of-scope per §3 but worth noting) could erode the cover. False positives in the Bloom mean some junk gets prioritized too (acceptable). Adds beacon bandwidth. SOUL-PRESERVING fallback if this bend is unacceptable: keep eviction fully random but bias RETENTION (not spreading) by 'blob age since last seen as new' — locally-measurable, leaks nothing, no beacon.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Recency-biased retention + freshness-priority spreading (purely local, leaks nothing)
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Attacks 2 and 3 — claw back delivery probability under buffer pressure using only local, content-blind signals.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Two local heuristics that need no identity, no destination, no beacon: (1) Spreading priority by freshness: when a contact window is short, prioritize re-broadcasting the blobs you've held SHORTEST (newest to you) — these are the ones least likely to have already saturated the crowd, so each transmission yields max new coverage. A fresh real blob thus rides a priority wave outward. Cover/dummy blobs are minted fresh too, so this doesn't distinguish real from cover. (2) Retention under pressure: when evicting, keep random eviction for the OLD bulk (anti-abuse, §8) but exempt a small 'newcomer' tranche (blobs seen in the last N minutes) from eviction, so a just-injected real blob gets a guaranteed minimum number of spread cycles before it competes in the random-eviction pool. Bound the exempt tranche size so a flooder can't exploit it (it's a fixed fraction, refilled by ANY new blobs including their junk — so it self-limits).
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Materially raises the probability that a new real blob reaches the recipient before random eviction can drop it, partially neutralizing Attack 2 — WITHOUT any per-source identity or rate-limit and WITHOUT any wire metadata. Pure local policy. Complements rateless-IBLT reconciliation (the newcomers are exactly the reconciled delta).
constructiveAlternatives.cost: A flooder can stuff the 'newcomer' tranche with junk, diluting protection (mitigated by capping the tranche as a fraction and combining with §8 per-neighbor caps). Slightly favors new spam over old real messages near end-of-TTL — but those are dying anyway. Modest bookkeeping (per-blob first-seen timestamp, already needed for TTL).

constructiveAlternatives.title: Density/connectivity self-estimation → honest reach UX and adaptive cover floor (turn the cliff into a visible dial)
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Attacks 1, 5, 6 — make the percolation cliff and short-TTL penalty VISIBLE so users and the protocol can react before silent total failure.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Each node can locally estimate its mean reachable-neighbor degree (count distinct relaying peers per window) — a cheap, content-blind measurement. Compare against the known continuum-percolation critical degree (~4.5 for 2D disk; use a conservative engineering margin). If below threshold, the app: (a) warns 'mesh is sparse — your message may not reach beyond people near you' (extends §10's density-aware UX from a privacy warning into a DELIVERABILITY warning, closing Attack 6); (b) suggests a longer TTL so the blob has time to be ferried (Attack 1 mitigation, ties to the ferrying alternative); (c) on iOS, surfaces 'keep app in foreground for reliable relaying' (Attack 5); (d) optionally raises the cover-traffic floor to keep the k-anonymity set from collapsing in the sparse regime (the §10 floor becomes density-adaptive). This doesn't FIX sparsity but converts a silent binary failure into a managed, communicated degradation — and gives the protocol a local trigger to lean on ferrying/longer-range transports.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Neighbor-count is the single most predictive local signal for both deliverability (percolation) AND anonymity (cover set size), and it costs nothing to measure. Honest UX is the difference between 'user knows their protest message might not arrive' and 'user assumes it arrived and it didn't.' Provides the trigger condition for every other sparse-regime mitigation.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Neighbor-count estimation can be gamed by an adversary spawning many fake relaying identities to make a victim BELIEVE the mesh is healthy (Sybil at the link layer) — so the warning is advisory, not a guarantee. Adds UI surface and the risk of warning-fatigue. Doesn't move a single blob by itself.

creativeIdeas: Treat reach as a RATELESS problem end-to-end: not just rateless-IBLT reconciliation, but consider erasure-coding a single 255-char message into k fixed-size soup blobs where any m<k suffice to reconstruct (fountain code). In a lossy, eviction-heavy crowd, the recipient only needs to collect ENOUGH fragments, not the specific one — dramatically raising delivery probability under random eviction (directly fights Attack 2). All fragments are uniform opaque blobs; the recipient trial-decrypts and, on the m-th success, reconstructs. Cost: k-fold storage amplification per message (tighten via small k), and the fragments share a recipient-recognizable trait only AFTER trial-decrypt, so uniformity holds on the wire. Preserves all invariants; worth modeling the storage/delivery tradeoff.

Diurnal/mobility 'breathing' of the cover floor: humans cluster and disperse predictably (rush hour, end-of-event stadium exodus). Schedule cover-traffic and aggressive-ferry-retention to ANTICIPATE dispersal — right before a crowd breaks up (detectable as falling neighbor count), nodes max out soup retention so the dispersing crowd becomes a fan of ferries radiating outward. The crowd's own predictable dissolution becomes the delivery mechanism. Blind, no routing, no internet.

Geographic-but-blind spreading via coarse, hashed locality buckets WITHOUT GPS or destinations: a node could weight retention slightly toward blobs it has seen propagate across MORE distinct radio environments (a cheap proxy for 'this blob is travelling / wants to travel'), measured purely from the set of neighbor-fingerprints it co-occurred with — never position, never destination. Speculative and risks a fingerprinting surface; flagged as soul-bending-adjacent, include only if the want-beacon idea is rejected.

Reframe TTL as a delivery BUDGET, not just a death timer: show the user an honest estimate ('at current density, 1 min ≈ reaches people within ~2 hops; 1 hour ≈ likely reaches the whole square; 1 day ≈ may ferry to nearby districts'). Same signed absolute TTL (invariant 5 untouched), but the UX teaches the spreading-vs-clock race so users pick TTLs that actually deliver. Cheap, honest, no protocol change.

Percolation-aware 'gathering health' shared signal: since neighbor-degree is locally measurable and the critical threshold is a known constant, the app could show a simple, local 'mesh strength' meter (like signal bars) — pure local computation, no network reporting — so a protest organizer can SEE when the crowd has thinned below the delivery cliff and physically re-densify (ask people to bunch up). Human action closes the percolation gap; the protocol just makes the invisible threshold visible.

Pair the active-delete ack channel's existing 'ordinary sealed message' mechanism (§7) with implicit delivery-density feedback to the SENDER only: since the sender already learns delivery via the private ack, the absence of an ack within a fraction of the TTL is a (sender-private) signal that density was insufficient — prompting 'resend with longer TTL?' No new wire metadata; reuses the §7 channel; keeps the recipient silent.

techniques.name: Practical Rateless Set Reconciliation (Yang, Gilad, Alizadeh — SIGCOMM 2024; arXiv:2402.02668; open-source Go 'riblt')
techniques.relevance: The right primitive to replace §15.3 Bloom have/want gossip. Reconciles in cost ∝ set DIFFERENCE not set size (~1.35x overhead, O(log d)/element, 2–2000x less compute than prior schemes), and is RATELESS so it adapts to variable-length BLE contacts. Directly addresses Attack 3 and frees contact bandwidth for actual blob spreading. Operates on opaque IDs only — no invariant cost.

techniques.name: Minisketch / PinSketch (BCH-based set reconciliation, used in Bitcoin Erlay)
techniques.relevance: Battle-tested set-reconciliation alternative for small, bounded differences; relevant if the per-contact delta is reliably small. Erlay is the canonical 'reconcile a gossip mempool efficiently' precedent. Rateless IBLT generally dominates for variable/unknown difference sizes, but Minisketch is simpler and proven in production.

techniques.name: Continuum percolation theory (Gilbert disk model; critical mean degree ~4.5 in 2D)
techniques.relevance: Quantifies the cliff in Attack 1 and grounds the density-self-estimation alternative: connectivity is a sharp phase transition in mean reachable-neighbor degree, so neighbor-count is the actionable local signal. The ~4.5 figure is the commonly cited 2D unit-disk value — treat as order-of-magnitude, verify against the specific mobility/range model before hard-coding a threshold.

techniques.name: Spray-and-Wait & Epidemic Routing in DTNs (Spyropoulos et al., 2005; Vahdat & Becker)
techniques.relevance: The deliverability-vs-overhead frontier for intermittently-connected sparse networks. meldingx is pure epidemic (max delivery, max overhead). The ferrying and freshness-priority alternatives borrow the INSIGHT (mobility + buffering beats sparsity) while deliberately discarding the per-message copy-budget/routing that those schemes use and that v0.1 correctly rejected for leaking the source.

techniques.name: Bloom-filter / decoyed private-membership rendezvous (PSI-lite literature, e.g. garbled/encrypted Bloom-filter PSI)
techniques.relevance: Basis for the 'interest without revealing interest' want-beacon. Note: eprint 2024/1901 documents real insecurities in naive Bloom-filter PSI — so the want-beacon should rely on k-anonymity via decoy padding (cheap, phone-friendly) rather than claiming cryptographic PSI guarantees. Mark as a design pattern, not a hardened protocol.

techniques.name: Fountain / LT / Raptor erasure codes (Luby et al.)
techniques.relevance: Foundation for the 'message as m-of-k uniform fragments' creative idea — lets a recipient reconstruct from ANY sufficient subset, raising delivery odds under random eviction (Attack 2) while every fragment stays a uniform opaque blob on the wire.

techniques.name: Bitchat (BLE managed-flooding + store-and-forward caching) and Meshtastic/LoRa companion pattern
techniques.relevance: Real-world precedent for store-carry-forward on phones and for using a longer-range radio (LoRa, km-scale) to bridge BLE islands — directly supports the opportunistic-longer-range-hop alternative for the blackout-rural cliff, with the same opaque-blob flood. Confirms feasibility; note their weaker anonymity model is NOT being imported, only the transport-bridging idea.

techniques.name: Wi-Fi Aware (Neighbor Awareness Networking, NAN) publish/subscribe
techniques.relevance: Higher-range (~100m+), higher-throughput transport the spec already defers (§15). Running the identical soup over NAN multiplies effective neighbor degree (~100x area per ~10x range), the most direct physics-level mitigation for the percolation cliff and the iOS-BLE-throttle problem; its MAC/fingerprint surface needs the §10 hardening (verify current iOS/Android NAN support before committing).

analyses.dimension: CRYPTO — envelope/handshake soundness for v0.2 (prekeys & FS in an async serverless soup, which-prekey selection, transcript binding, 256-bit message-ID, PQ-hybrid agility, hash(secret) delete)
analyses.assessment: The crypto is sober and well-read for a v0.2 vision doc — sealed-sender with identity-inside-the-seal, mandatory fail-closed inner signatures, a full-length signed transcript a la Davis-2001, full 256-bit non-truncatable message-ID, and version-bound agility are all the right instincts and put it ahead of Bridgefy-class designs. But the spec papers over the single hardest crypto problem the architecture creates, and it is a direct consequence of two invariants colliding: pure flooding (no routing) + sealed-sender (no recipient hint on the wire) means EVERY recipient must trial-decrypt EVERY blob against EVERY one of their valid keys, AND there is no server to hand out / retire one-time prekeys. The X3DH prekey machinery the spec borrows assumes a server that atomically deletes a used prekey and selects which one to serve; in a serverless soup neither exists. The result is that the forward-secrecy story is weaker than stated (the static-key fallback is not a corner case — it is the steady state once a contact's small prekey batch is consumed offline, and the X3DH replay weakness on the no-OTPK path applies directly), and "which prekey did the sender pick" becomes a live correctness and side-channel question. Two concrete crypto bugs are latent: (a) ML-KEM's documented lack of MAL-BIND-K-CT/PK means the planned X-Wing hybrid must be wired so the binding goes through SHA3 (X-Wing gives this, raw KEM-combine-it-yourself does not) or trial-decrypt can be made ambiguous; (b) the hash(secret) delete is a 256-bit-preimage MAC-with-no-key for a public verifier and has a grinding/shadowing surface the spec flags but does not close. None of these break the soul; all have soul-preserving fixes, and one of them (puncturable encryption) actually fits the serverless model FAR better than X3DH prekeys do. My honest bottom line: the envelope crypto is sound in isolation but the KEY-MANAGEMENT layer was specified for a client-server world and needs to be re-derived for a serverless soup before it is buildable.

attacks.title: Prekey exhaustion is the steady state, not the corner case → FS silently degrades to static
attacks.mechanism: X3DH one-time prekeys (OTPKs) assume a server that hands out a fresh prekey per first-message and deletes it. In a serverless OOB-pairing model, the recipient hands a peer a FINITE signed batch of OTPKs once, at pairing, and can never replenish them offline (replenishment IS an online channel). After N messages from a given sender (or N senders each grabbing one), that contact is out of OTPKs and every subsequent message falls to the static-key fallback. The spec treats fallback as a 'UX warning' edge; in a long-lived blackout it is the normal mode. Worse: there is no server to enforce one-OTPK-per-message, so two senders (or one sender replaying) can both seal to the SAME OTPK index, and the recipient cannot tell prekey-reuse from legitimate first-contact. A seized static key then decrypts the entire fallback corpus — exactly the harvest-now-decrypt-later loss FS was supposed to prevent.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 5 (ephemerality/FS as a real guarantee) and the §4/§12 FS claim; quietly, not loudly.

attacks.title: X3DH no-OTPK replay applies directly to the soup and is amplified by flooding
attacks.mechanism: Signal's own X3DH spec warns that a message NOT using a one-time prekey can be replayed and accepted, making the recipient think the sender sent it repeatedly. In Signal the Double Ratchet's first reply injects fresh randomness and kills the replay; meldingx is store-and-forward fire-and-forget with NO ratchet and NO reply required, so the only replay defenses are the seen-record Bloom + timestamp window. But the soup is a replay AMPLIFIER: the same ciphertext legitimately reappears from thousands of relays, so the recipient's dedup MUST be keyed on message-ID. If an attacker can produce a DIFFERENT message-ID for the same underlying static-fallback ciphertext (e.g. by exploiting any malleability or by re-wrapping), the Bloom record is bypassed and the static-fallback message replays into the inbox after the original purged.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 5 (self-destruct/anti-resurrection) and §5.2 replay/dedup; also pollutes the inbox-disappear-after-read guarantee.

attacks.title: Trial-decrypt ambiguity via ML-KEM non-binding once X-Wing/PQ lands
attacks.mechanism: The whole soup model rests on 'AEAD verify succeeds ⇒ this blob is mine, and it decodes to exactly one plaintext/sender.' ML-KEM is documented to LACK MAL-BIND-K-CT and MAL-BIND-K-PK: a malicious party can craft material where the same shared secret binds to more than one (ciphertext, pubkey). If the PQ hybrid is built by naively concatenating an X25519 secret with a raw ML-KEM secret and feeding AEAD, an attacker who knows the recipient's prekey can construct two distinct envelopes that BOTH trial-decrypt successfully for the recipient but carry different inner sender identities / message-IDs — letting a censor mint a blob that the victim accepts as 'from Alice' or that collides message-IDs in the seen-record (resurrection/suppression). X-Wing specifically fixes this (it satisfies MAL-BIND-K,CT-PK because the shared secret is hashed with both the ML-KEM ciphertext and the X25519 transcript through SHA3-256); a hand-rolled combiner does not.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 3 (uniformity: trial-decrypt must give an unambiguous mine/not-mine) and Invariant 4 (sealed-sender authenticity).

attacks.title: hash(secret) is an unkeyed public-verifier MAC → grinding / ID-shadowing / suppression
attacks.mechanism: message-ID = hash(secret) and the active-delete is 'broadcast secret; anyone checks hash(secret)==ID and purges.' Because verification is public and unkeyed, three problems: (1) ID-shadowing — an attacker grinds secret' until hash(secret') shares a Bloom-bucket signature with a victim's live ID, then either pre-seeds that ID into everyone's seen-record (so the victim's real message is rejected as 'already seen' = pre-emptive suppression) or mints a delete that purges a partial-collision class. Bloom membership is a SET of bits, so near-collisions matter, not just full 256-bit ones. (2) Once secret is broadcast to delete, ID=hash(secret) is now a publicly known constant forever; combined with the 30-day seen-record this is a durable, linkable, sender-attributable token (a sniffer that logged the original blob now learns 'that specific blob was a real message that got deleted' — the very 'this was real' signal §12 admits). (3) Hostile relay suppression: a relay that sees secret can purge faster and more widely than honest TTL, or withhold/replay it to desync deletion. The spec lists all three under open-question §13.7 but ships no construction.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 5 (delete forge-resistance / no-resurrection) and Invariant 3 (the deleted-blob becomes distinguishable as 'was real').

attacks.title: Transcript-binding deniability vs sealed-sender authenticity are in tension
attacks.mechanism: The signed transcript binds sender_pk‖recipient_pk‖text and the inner signature is mandatory/fail-closed. That is great for surreptitious-forwarding resistance (Davis) but it gives the recipient a NON-repudiable, transferable proof that 'sender_pk said text to recipient_pk' — a seized/coerced recipient phone yields cryptographic evidence usable against the sender, in exactly the protest/seizure threat model this tool targets. Signal/X3DH deliberately use a DH-based MAC (deniable, both parties could have forged it), not a transferable signature. By upgrading to a real Ed25519 signature over the content for forwarding-resistance, the design traded away the offline deniability that the threat model (device seizure, coercion) most needs.
attacks.breaks: Not a named invariant but a direct hit on the §3/§7 seizure-and-coercion posture; arguably undercuts Invariant 4's spirit (the 'from' should not be PROVABLE to third parties).

attacks.title: Which-prekey selection is itself a deanonymization / linkage side channel
attacks.mechanism: Sealed-sender means the wire carries no recipient hint, so a sender seals to ONE specific recipient prekey. But the recipient must trial-decrypt against ALL of their own outstanding prekey private keys for EVERY blob in the soup. Two leaks: (1) timing — the index that succeeds, or the position in the trial loop, is observable via cache/timing if the implementation isn't constant-pass; more importantly the COST scales as (live-blobs × my-prekeys), which is the §13.5 cost bomb and also a battery/uniformity tell (a recipient with many active conversations burns measurably more CPU). (2) If prekeys are consumed in order and the recipient ever republishes a fresh batch out-of-band, the gap structure (which indices were used) can correlate senders across pairings. There is no server to blind any of this.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 3 (uniformity — CPU/power signature must be charge-independent and message-independent per §10) and Invariant 4.

attacks.title: Cover/null blobs must trial-decrypt-fail for EVERYONE, or they leak
attacks.mechanism: Cover traffic = 'sealed-to-null' dummies that must propagate exactly like real msgs and must NEVER trial-decrypt-succeed for any honest node. If a dummy is just 'AEAD over random bytes with a random ephemeral_pk,' fine — but it must still carry a valid PoW and a well-formed signed core, or relays can fast-drop dummies as malformed (distinguisher → uniformity break). Conversely if the dummy's core is signed by a real identity it links. The spec says sealed-to-nobody but never specifies what key signs the dummy's immutable core; if the answer is 'no inner signature,' then the presence/absence of a verifiable inner sig becomes the real/cover distinguisher for anyone who can ever decrypt (i.e. the intended recipient sees 'this one had no inner content' — that's fine — but a node holding a leaked key could distinguish). Needs a precise definition.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 3 (cover indistinguishable from real).

constructiveAlternatives.title: Replace one-time prekeys with PUNCTURABLE ENCRYPTION (Green–Miers, NDSS/CS&P 2015) — single static public key, server-free, self-retiring
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Prekey exhaustion as steady state; which-prekey selection leak; replenishment-needs-a-server problem; trial-decrypt cost.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Puncturable encryption gives the recipient ONE long-lived public key (perfect for OOB QR exchange — exchange once, never replenish) and a private key the recipient 'punctures' after reading each message so that key can no longer decrypt that ciphertext, WITHOUT any sender interaction or server. The sender just encrypts to the single public key + a tag (e.g. the message-ID or an epoch); there is NO prekey to select, NO batch to exhaust, NO index to reuse — which kills attacks #1, #6, and the static-fallback entirely. Forward secrecy is per-message and automatic on read (matches 'disappear-after-read'). Practical concern: classic Green–Miers puncturing grows key size with #punctures; modern bounded/epoch-based variants and the libforwardsec implementation cap this by puncturing per short epoch (e.g. per hour, aligned to the ≤7d TTL) rather than per message, so key size stays bounded — a clean fit for an already-time-bounded system.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Eliminates the single weakest crypto subsystem (serverless prekey lifecycle) and makes FS the default rather than 'only if prekeys are used.' One static public key per identity also simplifies the QR/OOB exchange.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Higher per-decrypt cost and larger keys than X25519; pairing-based or lattice variants add a dependency and need a careful constant-time impl. Marks a real research-to-engineering lift. Confidence the primitive exists and fits: well-grounded (this is literally the problem PE was invented for); confidence it is cheap enough on a phone at soup volume: plausible-to-speculative — must benchmark.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Receiver-derived prekey lattice from a single seed (HKDF tree) — keep X3DH shape, kill exhaustion & reuse
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Prekey exhaustion; OTPK reuse; serverless replenishment; static fallback.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Instead of a finite signed batch, the recipient publishes (OOB) one static X25519 'master prekey' M and a signed epoch schedule. The effective prekey for epoch e is PK_e = X25519(base, HKDF(seed, e)) where the recipient knows seed; the sender derives PK_e deterministically from M and the epoch counter (epoch = floor((now)/Δ), Δ ~ minutes). The recipient deletes the epoch private key once that epoch's window + max-TTL has passed → forward secrecy without a server and without ever running out (epochs are unbounded). Sender selects 'which prekey' purely from the clock, so there is no index to reuse and nothing to leak about the recipient's conversation count. Replay across epochs is naturally bounded by epoch-key deletion. This is essentially a coarse, sender-passive ratchet keyed on time rather than on message order, which is exactly what a no-reply store-and-forward system can support.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Minimal deviation from the already-specified X3DH-style design; pure X25519 (no new heavy primitive); bounded recipient state (only ~ TTL/Δ live epoch keys); kills exhaustion AND the reuse ambiguity in one move.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Requires loose clock sync between sender and recipient (epoch must overlap with TTL window — but the envelope already carries a signed creation-timestamp, so the sender's claimed epoch is self-describing and the recipient can trial-decrypt against the 2–3 epochs around the timestamp, cheaply). FS granularity = epoch, not per-message (weaker than puncturable but far stronger than static fallback).

constructiveAlternatives.title: Make message-ID a KEYED commitment, not a bare hash(secret): ID = H(domain‖secret), delete-token = separate value
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: hash(secret) grinding/shadowing/suppression (#4); deleted-blob becomes-distinguishable.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Three concrete fixes that stack: (1) Bind the ID to the envelope so it can't be ground independently of a valid message: message-ID = H(domain_sep‖secret‖ephemeral_pk‖creation-ts). Now an attacker can't pre-grind an ID that will collide a victim's FUTURE message because the victim's ephemeral_pk/ts aren't known in advance — pre-emptive seen-record poisoning dies. (2) Decouple the delete authority from the ID: let delete-token = H(domain_delete‖secret) be a SEPARATE preimage, so revealing the delete-token to purge does NOT reveal anything that was on the wire, and logging the original blob never lets you confirm 'this got deleted' (the on-wire ID and the delete-token are different hashes of secret under different domains — you can't link them without secret). (3) Require the delete to carry the original signed core (or a sender re-signature over delete-token‖message-ID) so a relay that merely sniffs secret still cannot purge more aggressively than the sender authorized, and so the delete is itself dedup/anti-replay-protected.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Closes §13.7 grinding/shadowing/suppression with no new primitive — just domain separation + binding the ID to envelope-unique fields. Keeps the elegant 'only sender+recipient know secret' property and the structural-wall reasoning intact.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Trivial compute; one extra hash and a slightly larger delete message. The Bloom near-collision concern (#4 part 1) still needs separate sizing (see techniques: use ID = full 256-bit and a low-FP Bloom, and consider a counting/partitioned filter so an attacker can't cheaply hit a target bucket).

constructiveAlternatives.title: Adopt X-Wing as the HYBRID (not a hand-rolled combiner) and bind the ML-KEM ciphertext into the AEAD AAD
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Trial-decrypt ambiguity / ML-KEM non-binding (#3); PQ agility correctness.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: X-Wing's shared secret = SHA3-256(ss_ML-KEM ‖ ss_X25519 ‖ ct_X25519 ‖ pk_X25519 ‖ label). Because the KEM ciphertext and pubkey are hashed INTO the secret, X-Wing achieves MAL-BIND-K,CT-PK that raw ML-KEM lacks — i.e. a given derived key binds to exactly one (ciphertext, recipient-pubkey), which is precisely the property the trial-decrypt 'is this uniquely mine' test needs. Concrete rule for the spec: (a) never roll your own ML-KEM combiner; use X-Wing verbatim; (b) additionally put the FULL KEM ciphertext + ephemeral_pk + message-ID into the AEAD AAD so the AEAD tag itself commits to them (defense in depth = the AEAD becomes key-committing over the wire fields too); (c) keep running BOTH components always (already specified) and bind version into the transcript (already specified). This turns the PQ migration from a latent binding bug into a strict upgrade.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Makes 'AEAD-verify ⇒ unambiguously mine, from exactly one sender, exactly one ID' provably true even under malicious KEM material — the linchpin assumption of the whole soup. Uses a CFRG-track, analyzed construction rather than DIY.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: X-Wing ciphertext/pubkey are ML-KEM-768-sized (~1.1KB ct, ~1.2KB pk) — this BLOWS the 255-char/~1KB fixed envelope. Mitigation: define TWO fixed bucket sizes (classical-only and hybrid) and accept that hybrid mode shrinks the anonymity set to 'hybrid users'; OR ship hybrid as a larger uniform bucket once PQ is the default for everyone, never mixed. This is a real uniformity-vs-PQ tension to decide deliberately (see creative idea on bucketing).

constructiveAlternatives.title: Swap the transferable Ed25519 content-signature for a DENIABLE authenticator (DH-MAC now; K-Waay-style ring/designated-verifier for PQ)
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Transcript-binding kills offline deniability (#5) under seizure/coercion.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Keep the FULL transcript (domain‖version‖sender_pk‖recipient_pk‖ID‖ephemeral_pk‖TTL‖ts‖text) but authenticate it with a key that BOTH parties could have computed — i.e. a MAC under a key derived from the sender↔recipient DH (sealed-sender already establishes a shared secret), exactly as Signal does for deniability. This still defeats surreptitious-forwarding/signature-lifting (the MAC is bound to recipient_pk and the transcript, so it's invalid if re-sealed to a third party) WITHOUT producing a third-party-transferable proof that the sender said it. A seized recipient phone can fabricate the same MAC, so it proves nothing to a coercer. For the PQ/agility era where you also want post-compromise authentication, K-Waay (eprint 2024/120) is a real, recent construction giving deniable post-quantum X3DH WITHOUT ring signatures — a candidate to track rather than hand-rolling. Note the soul-tension: deniability vs the inner-signature-mandatory rule. The MAC IS still mandatory and fail-closed; we only change it from 'signature (transferable)' to 'MAC (deniable)'.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Restores the deniability the threat model (protest, device seizure, coercion) most needs, while keeping forwarding-resistance and fail-closed authentication. Aligns with Signal's deliberate choice.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Bends the letter of Invariant 4's 'identity+signature INSIDE the seal' — it's now a MAC, not a signature, so you lose non-repudiation (which here is a FEATURE). If you genuinely need the recipient to be CERTAIN-to-themselves which key signed (not just authenticate), keep an inner signature but over a DELIBERATELY non-incriminating value, or use a designated-verifier signature (deniable to third parties, convincing to the recipient). SOUL-PRESERVING fallback if you reject deniability changes: keep Ed25519 but document loudly that recipient-seizure yields transferable proof, and let the user pick per-message 'deniable' vs 'signed-receipt' mode.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Constant-work trial-decrypt: cheap symmetric 'mine?' tag derived from a per-epoch shared secret, gating the expensive AEAD
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Trial-decrypt cost bomb (#6 / §13.5); the CPU/power uniformity tell.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Today every blob costs a full X25519 + AEAD trial per recipient key. Add a cheap precomputable filter that does NOT add a wire distinguisher: for each contact and epoch, both ends derive a short rotating 'attention tag' set; the sender includes one tag (drawn from a per-pair pseudorandom sequence) in the encrypted-but-fast-checkable position. The recipient precomputes the expected tags for its contacts for the current epoch into a local hash-set and does an O(1) lookup per blob; only on a HIT does it run the expensive X25519+AEAD. CRITICAL constraint to preserve uniformity: the tag must be (a) the same byte-length and entropy as random for non-contacts (cover/relay blobs carry random tags), so the wire is uniform, and (b) NOT a stable per-recipient value (rotate per epoch from a shared PRF) so it isn't a recipient selector for an outside observer — it's only meaningful to someone who already shares the per-pair secret. This is a private-set-intersection-flavored optimization, not a wire-visible address.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Drops steady-state work from O(blobs × my-keys × full-DH) to O(blobs × O(1) lookup), flattening the battery/CPU signature toward charge-independent uniformity (§10) and making the design buildable at 1–2M live blobs.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Bends Invariant 4 slightly: there is now a field whose VALUE is meaningful to the intended recipient — if mis-designed it becomes exactly the 'per-message targeting metadata' the no-routing invariant forbids. Must be proven indistinguishable-from-random to anyone without the per-pair secret (i.e. it is a covert tag, not an address). SOUL-PRESERVING fallback that bends nothing: precompute/batch the X25519 shared-secret half (sender's ephemeral_pk is on the wire; recipient can batch-multiply) and use AEAD-only as the test — already a large speedup with zero new fields, just no O(1) prefilter.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Precise cover-blob and null-seal definition: dummies are real AEAD over random plaintext with a throwaway identity that DOES sign its core
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Cover/real distinguisher via inner-signature presence (#7).
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Define a cover blob as: generate a one-shot throwaway Ed25519/X25519 keypair, run the IDENTICAL envelope construction (fresh ephemeral_pk, real PoW over the immutable core, valid inner signature by the throwaway key, AEAD over uniformly random plaintext sealed to a random/unknowable recipient pubkey), then DISCARD the throwaway keys. Result: a cover blob is byte-for-byte process-identical to a real one — valid PoW, valid signed core, valid AEAD — and trial-decrypts-fail for everyone (no one holds the random recipient key). The ONLY difference is semantic (no one can open it), which is unobservable on the wire and even unobservable to a relay. This also means even a node that LEAKS a key learns nothing distinguishing from cover, because cover is sealed to keys that never existed.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Closes the cover/real gap rigorously and makes the 'sealed to nobody' phrase precise and implementable; cover blobs now satisfy every check a relay can run.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Cover blobs cost a real PoW + signature to mint, which is the intended price of uniformity but adds to the §10 cover-traffic CPU budget (interacts with the pre-mined-PoW pool design). Negligible storage.

creativeIdeas: Two-tier fixed buckets for the PQ era: a 'classical' ~1KB bucket and a 'hybrid' ~3KB bucket, with a hard sunset date after which ONLY the hybrid bucket is emitted (everyone moves together). This preserves uniformity WITHIN each tier and avoids a permanent two-population anonymity split — the price (one flag day) is acceptable and is the honest way to reconcile X-Wing's size with the fixed-envelope invariant. Crucially, never let a user MIX tiers, or the choice itself becomes a fingerprint.

Time-as-the-ratchet: because the envelope already carries a signed creation-timestamp and an absolute TTL, lean into time-keyed epoch prekeys (alt #2) and time-keyed cover-traffic shaping together — the same epoch clock that rotates prekeys can drive the diurnal cover-traffic model (§13.3), so 'which key' and 'when to emit dummies' share one bounded, sync-free schedule.

Self-describing crypto-suite via the version field as a tiny capability vector (KEM-id‖sig-id‖kdf-id), all bound into the transcript, so a recipient trial-decrypts with the RIGHT primitive without any negotiation round-trip — negotiation is impossible in fire-and-forget anyway. This makes agility a pure local-dispatch problem and pre-empts downgrade by binding the full suite, not just a monotonic version number.

Key-committing AEAD everywhere (XChaCha20-Poly1305 is NOT key-committing by default): add an explicit commitment (e.g. the first 32 bytes of HKDF over the key, or use a committing AEAD construction / Encrypt-then-HMAC over the key) so a single ciphertext cannot be made to open under two keys. This is cheap insurance against the whole class of 'one blob trial-decrypts for two different recipients/senders' ambiguities that the soup model is uniquely sensitive to, and it composes with the X-Wing binding fix.

Per-pair 'inbox epoch' nonces to make replay structurally impossible without a server: derive the AEAD nonce from a per-pair PRF over (epoch, counter) the recipient also tracks; a replayed ciphertext after the epoch's key is punctured/deleted simply won't decrypt, giving anti-resurrection from the crypto layer in addition to the Bloom seen-record (defense in depth for attack #2).

Treat the delete-token as a hash-CHAIN anchor: secret = tip of a short hash chain, message-ID = H(chain_root‖envelope-fields). The sender can reveal successive chain links to authorize partial actions (e.g. 'soft expire now' vs 'hard purge') without ever revealing the root, giving graded, forge-resistant delete semantics from one preimage primitive — a creative extension of the hash(secret) idea that keeps its minimalism.

Borrow RLN's Shamir-secret-sharing punishment as the anonymous rate-limit primitive but anchor membership in the OOB pairing graph instead of an on-chain Merkle tree: each identity gets a per-epoch share budget; exceeding it reconstructs a slashing secret that lets ANY honest node drop that emitter's future blobs — no global registry, no identity on the wire, membership proven by a ZK proof of being SOME paired contact. This is speculative and heavy for a phone, but it's the one primitive that could turn §13.1 from open to tractable while preserving anonymity-via-uniformity.

techniques.name: Green & Miers, 'Forward Secure Asynchronous Messaging from Puncturable Encryption' (IEEE S&P 2015; cs.umd.edu/~imiers/pdf/forwardsec.pdf, impl: github.com/imichaelmiers/libforwardsec)
techniques.relevance: The canonical serverless, single-public-key, self-retiring forward-secrecy primitive — directly replaces the broken serverless prekey lifecycle (attack #1/#6). This is the strongest structural fix.

techniques.name: Signal X3DH spec, replay note (signal.org/docs/specifications/x3dh)
techniques.relevance: Confirms verbatim that no-one-time-prekey messages are replayable and accepted — i.e. the static-fallback path in meldingx inherits a known replay weakness with no Double-Ratchet to close it (attack #2).

techniques.name: X-Wing hybrid KEM (eprint 2024/039; CFRG draft draft-connolly-cfrg-xwing-kem; CiC 2024)
techniques.relevance: Provides MAL-BIND-K,CT-PK and LEAK-BIND properties that raw ML-KEM lacks; the correct off-the-shelf hybrid so trial-decrypt stays unambiguous post-PQ (attack #3). Sizes (~1.1KB ct) force the bucketing decision.

techniques.name: Deirdre Connolly, 'How to Hold KEMs' + 'ML-KEM Mythbusting' (durumcrustulum.com 2024; keymaterial.net 2025) and the MAL/LEAK/HON-BIND taxonomy
techniques.relevance: Source for ML-KEM's documented lack of MAL-BIND-K-CT/MAL-BIND-K-PK — the precise reason a hand-rolled combiner is unsafe for the soup's trial-decrypt assumption.

techniques.name: Key-committing AEAD / 'Partitioning Oracle' work (Len–Grubbs–Ristenpart, USENIX 2021) — note: I'm citing from memory, verify the exact venue
techniques.relevance: XChaCha20-Poly1305 is not key-committing; a multi-recipient trial-decrypt system is exactly where non-committing AEAD bites. Motivates the committing-AEAD creative idea. (Mark: venue from memory, confirm.)

techniques.name: K-Waay: 'Fast and Deniable Post-Quantum X3DH without Ring Signatures' (eprint 2024/120)
techniques.relevance: Real recent construction for DENIABLE post-quantum handshake — the route to restore deniability (attack #5) in the PQ era without the cost of ring signatures.

techniques.name: Hashimoto et al. 'An Efficient and Generic Construction for Signal's Handshake (X3DH): PQ, State-Leakage Secure, and Deniable' (PKC 2021 / eprint 2021/616)
techniques.relevance: Formal treatment of X3DH deniability + state-leakage (device-seizure) security — directly on-point for the seizure threat model and the deniability-vs-signature tension (#5).

techniques.name: RLN / Rate-Limiting Nullifier (rate-limiting-nullifier.github.io; Vac RFC 32; ethresear.ch/t/...5009) using Shamir secret sharing + Semaphore
techniques.relevance: Concrete anonymous-rate-limit primitive (Shamir-share-leak-on-overuse) the spec already names; the creative idea adapts its punishment mechanism to the OOB pairing graph for §13.1.

techniques.name: Davis, 'Defective Sign & Encrypt in S/MIME, PKCS#7...' (USENIX 2001) — already cited in spec as the transcript-binding motivation
techniques.relevance: Correctly motivates binding sender/recipient into the signed transcript; the caveat is that doing it with a transferable SIGNATURE rather than a deniable MAC creates the seizure-deniability problem (#5).

analyses.dimension: LIFETIME — Lifetime/retention & storage: the three knobs, 30-day Bloom seen-record, bounded buffer, eviction, panic/duress
analyses.assessment: The LIFETIME subsystem is the most carefully thought-through part of the spec and also the one whose load-bearing assumptions are most quietly fragile. The architecture is internally coherent: absolute signed TTL kills the immortal-message bug, attacker-uncorrelated eviction kills the v0.1 priority-queue inversion, and the Bloom seen-record correctly bounds trial-decrypt and replay. But three foundations are softer than the spec admits. (1) The entire death-guarantee is denominated in wall-clock time (creation-ts + TTL) on devices that, by invariant #1, have NO trusted time source — no internet, no NTP. Clock skew of tens of minutes is normal and clock manipulation is trivial; an attacker who sets their clock back makes messages effectively immortal on their own node, and an attacker who sets honest neighbors' perceived time forward (via the unauthenticated creation-ts they accept on inbound blobs — though that's signed) or simply runs a slow clock keeps spam alive past its intended death. The spec calls reset-on-hop the immortal-message bug but never names the clock-trust bug, which is the same bug one layer down. (2) The Bloom seen-record is the linchpin of anti-resurrection AND a memory-DoS amplifier AND a silent message-loss source via false positives — a single data structure carrying three conflicting jobs, and the spec's "1.2 bytes/ID, tens of MB" sizing assumes an HONEST ID population, which an attacker controls. (3) The relay-retention tragedy-of-the-commons is acknowledged as an open question but the proposed mitigation ("default generous, opt-in stingy") is a UX default, not a mechanism — it survives exactly as long as users don't notice their battery draining, i.e., not long. The eviction policy is the strongest piece but is still steerable under an adaptive flood because "retain-closest-to-delivery" has no notion of delivery without routing, so it degrades to "retain-closest-to-TTL," which is itself an attacker-controllable field (set a long TTL and your blobs are always the LAST evicted).

attacks.title: Clock-skew immortality: TTL is denominated in time the mesh cannot trust
attacks.mechanism: The death guarantee = local_clock >= creation_ts + global_TTL. By invariant #1 there is no internet/NTP, so each phone uses its own RTC. An attacker simply runs their node's clock slow (or sets it to the past): on that node every message's computed expiry is in the future forever, so the attacker becomes an immortal-blob reservoir that keeps re-broadcasting expired spam to honest neighbors. Honest neighbors will re-drop by their own (correct) clocks, but the attacker can keep re-seeding faster than they drop, and any honest phone whose RTC has drifted slow (common after a long blackout with a dead battery / coin-cell reset) silently extends every message it carries. There is no cross-check: creation-ts is signed by the SENDER (who may be the attacker), so a malicious sender can also backdate creation-ts to be honored, or forward-date it so honest nodes think the message is not yet expired. The spec's whole §6 Knob-1 argument assumes synchronized honest clocks and never states that assumption.
attacks.breaks: Invariant #5 (self-destruct / absolute TTL death guarantee); knock-on to #2 (immortal spam degrades the uniform soup).

attacks.title: Seen-record memory-DoS via attacker-chosen distinct IDs
attacks.mechanism: The 30-day Bloom is sized for honest traffic (~millions of IDs, tens of MB). But message-ID = hash(secret) and the attacker mints secrets for free off the send path. An attacker who floods 10^8-10^9 distinct well-formed-but-junk blobs (PoW is admitted to be defeatable 10^4-10^8x) forces every honest phone to insert every distinct ID it ever sees into its 30-day seen-record. A Bloom filter sized for 2M IDs at 1% FP holds ~10M before its FP rate explodes toward 1.0. Past that point EITHER the filter must grow without bound (memory DoS, OOM on the weakest battery phone = the defender) OR the FP rate climbs and the filter starts reporting 'already seen' for IDs the phone never saw — which silently drops REAL incoming messages (attack #3). Bloom filters cannot be resized in place, so growth means rebuild-from-scratch, a CPU/IO spike. The attacker thus has a free lever to convert flood volume directly into either memory exhaustion or honest-message loss on every phone in range.
attacks.breaks: Invariant #5/#7 (storage feasibility at gathering scale) and indirectly anonymity-of-delivery (real messages lost).

attacks.title: Seen-record false-positive censorship of a targeted message
attacks.mechanism: A Bloom filter false positive on an ID means 'I will refuse to accept this blob forever (for 30 days).' message-ID = hash(secret) and the attacker can grind secrets cheaply. To censor a SPECIFIC future message the attacker can't predict its ID, but to raise the GLOBAL FP rate against a victim phone the attacker floods it with distinct IDs until its filter saturates (attack #2), at which point ~1-in-X of ALL legitimate inbound IDs collide with a set bit and are silently rejected. Because rejection is indistinguishable from 'genuinely already seen,' the victim never knows messages are being dropped. Even with a perfectly-sized filter, an attacker who can observe or guess that a target blob's ID will be inserted can pre-poison: insert a colliding junk ID first so when the real blob arrives it reads as 'seen' and is dropped. No identity is needed for any of this.
attacks.breaks: Invariant #5 supporting machinery (anti-resurrection turns into a censorship primitive); reliable delivery.

attacks.title: TTL-field eviction steering (long-TTL blobs are last to die)
attacks.mechanism: The spec replaces 'least-PoW eviction' (correctly identified as bad) with randomized eviction + per-neighbor cap + 'retain-closest-to-delivery.' But with NO routing there is no 'closest-to-delivery' signal, so the only available proxy is 'closest-to-TTL' = evict the blobs about to die anyway, keep the freshest. global-TTL is an attacker-chosen field (up to 7d). An attacker stamps all their flood blobs with TTL=7d; every honest blob with a humane short TTL (the spec markets '1 min = right next to me') is therefore ALWAYS younger-relative-to-expiry and gets preferentially evicted under buffer pressure, while attacker blobs persist to the max. The eviction policy thus inverts into 'keep the longest-lived spam,' the mirror image of the v0.1 bug they thought they fixed. Randomized eviction blunts but does not remove this because per-blob survival probability still correlates with remaining-TTL if 'retain-closest-to-delivery' has any weight at all.
attacks.breaks: Invariant #2/#3 (attacker dominates the soup, degrading uniformity & honest delivery) and #5 (short-TTL feature becomes a delivery penalty).

attacks.title: Per-neighbor cap is bypassed by Sybil radios / MAC churn
attacks.mechanism: The §8 link-layer defense caps buffer-share per NEIGHBOR (<=15%/neighbor/window). But §10 mandates MAC randomization and ID-free advertisements specifically so neighbors are NOT stably identifiable. The same property that gives anonymity destroys the per-neighbor cap: an attacker rotates MAC/advertising identity every window and presents as N fresh 'neighbors,' each getting its own 15% share, so one physical radio captures ~N*15% of every honest buffer. The cap and the anonymity goal are in direct, unacknowledged tension. PHY-fingerprinting (which the threat model concedes the attacker has) would let an HONEST node coalesce a Sybil's radios — but honest nodes are not given a PHY fingerprinter, only the adversary is.
attacks.breaks: Invariant #3 (uniformity) vs the per-neighbor cap; lets a flooder dominate buffers.

attacks.title: Duress-wipe + rotate is a self-deanonymizing tell over time
attacks.mechanism: The panic delete-all dumps wiped IDs into the seen-record so neighbors can't re-seed them. But a persistent sensor net (in-model as the 'persistent wide-coverage adversary') sees a phone that, at time T, simultaneously (a) stops re-broadcasting a large correlated SET of IDs it was carrying and (b) the duress variant then re-appears as a fresh identity emitting cover traffic. The synchronized mass-drop of a known ID set is a far stronger fingerprint than any single active-delete (which §12 already flags as the lone metadata handle). Panic is exactly when the user can least afford a loud RF signature, yet the wipe creates the loudest possible one: 'this specific radio just dropped 1GB of soup at once.'
attacks.breaks: Invariant #3/#4 (uniformity / sealed-sender) against the persistent adversary at the worst moment.

attacks.title: Time-cap low-power mode is a covert immortality + partition lever
attacks.mechanism: Knob-2 secondary time-cap ('drop my copy after X min') is opt-in low-power. If an attacker can socially/UX-nudge a region of users into aggressive time-caps (or it becomes the de-facto default because battery anxiety is universal), the mesh's carrying capacity for any blob collapses below the percolation threshold and delivery craters — a denial-of-service achieved entirely through legitimate knob settings, no flooding required. Conversely the attacker themselves sets NO time cap and infinite size cap, becoming the only long-term reservoir, i.e., the de-facto router/observer of the whole soup.
attacks.breaks: Invariant #1/#7 (offline gathering-scale delivery) via knob abuse and the tragedy-of-the-commons.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Hop-counted relative TTL as a clock-independent SECONDARY death bound (belt-and-suspenders, not a replacement)
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Clock-skew immortality (attack #1)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Keep the signed absolute TTL exactly as designed (it stays the authoritative bound). ADD an unauthenticated, monotonically-DECREMENTED 'energy' counter in the mutable housekeeping region: each blob is born with a budget B (say 2^16) and every node that re-broadcasts it decrements by 1 (floored at 0; a node never increments — increment is structurally meaningless because it only shortens others' acceptance, never extends). A blob with energy 0 is locally dropped REGARDLESS of clock. This is the dual of the forbidden reset-on-hop: reset EXTENDS life and is attacker-incentivized, so it's banned; decrement SHORTENS life and is attacker-DIS-incentivized (a malicious relay gains nothing by decrementing faster, and decrementing slower only helps its own neighbors drop later, which the neighbor's own counter prevents). It is unauthenticated and that's fine because, like §5.1 housekeeping, it can only ever cause EARLIER death, never later — the safe direction. Result: even a node with a broken/backdated clock still bleeds blobs to death through hop-energy. Crucially this does NOT add routing or targeting — it's a scalar that travels with the blob, identical for real and cover.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Closes the silent clock-trust hole that the entire §6 argument rests on, with a 2-byte field and a decrement. Caps worst-case immortality at B hops even with zero trusted time.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Tunable-parameter risk: B too low partitions the mesh early (premature death), B too high is toothless. Needs the same care as TTL sizing. Adds 2 bytes to the fixed envelope (uniformity preserved since it's constant-size). A colluding clique could pass blobs in a loop to burn energy on victims — but that only kills blobs faster, the safe direction.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Gossip-median 'mesh epoch clock' for sanity-bounding TTL without NTP
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Clock-skew immortality (attack #1); knob abuse via forged creation-ts
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Borrow from Whonix sdwdate and threshold logical clocks (arXiv 1907.07010) and the digital-clock-trust SoK (arXiv 2502.09837). Each phone passively observes the creation-timestamps of the (signed) blobs it trial-fails on and maintains a robust estimator (median / trimmed mean) of 'the freshest creation-ts I've recently seen across many independent senders.' Because honest senders are many and independent, the high quantile of observed creation-ts tracks true wall-clock within minutes even with no NTP. A phone whose own RTC disagrees with the mesh-median by more than a threshold flags itself as clock-untrusted and (a) warns the user and (b) falls back to the hop-energy bound (alt above) for expiry decisions. This is purely passive, derives from data already on the wire, needs no new messages, no routing, no identity, and no targeting. It cannot be forced far off because skewing the median requires out-numbering honest fresh senders, which at gathering density is hard and is exactly the flooding the other defenses target.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Gives the TTL a trust-minimized clock reference in a fully offline mesh — the missing foundation under §6. Self-diagnoses drifted phones. Reuses the existing trial-decrypt stream, near-zero extra cost.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: A large flood of attacker blobs with forward-dated creation-ts could pull the median forward (making honest nodes expire honest messages EARLY — a censorship variant). Mitigate with a trimmed/low-quantile estimator and bounding the per-window adjustment rate (clamp drift to plausible physical RTC rates). Adds estimator state and a UX warning surface.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Replace the monolithic Bloom seen-record with a sliding-window / time-decaying filter (SWBF / cuckoo+timestamp / A2 buffering)
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Seen-record memory-DoS (#2) and false-positive censorship (#3)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: The 30-day seen-record only needs to outlive max-TTL (7d) with margin — it does NOT need 30 flat days of unbounded accumulation. Use a sliding-window Bloom filter (the SWBF construction: a cuckoo hash storing fingerprint+timestamp, arXiv 1712.01779 'Pay for a Sliding Bloom Filter') or the classic two-generation A2 active-aging Bloom (two filters, flip-and-clear the older when the active one fills). This gives (a) HARD bounded memory — when the window's capacity is hit you age out the OLDEST generation rather than degrading FP rate toward 1.0, so an ID-flood can never push FP above the design ceiling; it instead shortens the effective remembering window, and you size the window so 'shortened-but-still > maxTTL' holds even under flood. And (b) deletion support (cuckoo variant) so panic-wipe and explicit purge are exact, not approximate. The anti-resurrection guarantee becomes: 'an ID cannot be re-accepted within W, and W is provably >= maxTTL+margin even under maximal flood because aging is FIFO-by-time, not FP-by-saturation.'
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Converts the seen-record from an attacker-controlled memory/FP bomb into a hard-bounded structure with graceful, predictable degradation. Adds deletion. Directly fixes the spec's own open question §13.8.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Cuckoo/SWBF is ~2x the bits of a space-optimal Bloom and more complex; insertion can fail (needs a stash). Two-generation A2 doubles memory for the same window. Must prove window-under-flood >= maxTTL formally (now a concrete, tractable inequality).

constructiveAlternatives.title: Eviction by 'youngest-absolute-age' instead of 'closest-to-TTL' to neutralize the TTL-stamp steering attack
