constructiveAlternatives.addresses: TTL-field eviction steering (#4)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Make the primary eviction key the attacker-INDEPENDENT quantity (creation-ts, i.e. absolute wall-age) rather than the attacker-CONTROLLED quantity (remaining-TTL). Under buffer pressure, bias eviction toward the OLDEST-by-creation blobs (FIFO-ish), blended with uniform randomness. An attacker can no longer buy persistence by stamping TTL=7d, because survival no longer depends on declared TTL at all — only on real elapsed time, which they can't fake forward without also tripping the gossip-median clock check. Honest short-TTL blobs are no longer penalized: they die at their own TTL (Knob-1) regardless, and while alive they're treated identically to everything else of the same real age. Combine with the existing per-neighbor share cap and a small random component so no single field fully determines survival. Optionally weight a SMALL retention bonus to blobs whose ID you have NOT seen acked/dropped — but only via the clock-independent signals, never via routing.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Removes the declared-TTL knob as an eviction-priority lever; restores the v0.1-bug fix to actually hold. Short-TTL feature stops being a delivery penalty.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Pure-FIFO-by-age slightly hurts the freshest legitimate blobs' propagation head-start; the random blend mitigates. 'Youngest vs oldest' must be tuned against delivery latency (you still want new real messages to spread).

constructiveAlternatives.title: Proof-of-storage-time reciprocity: earn buffer-share by demonstrably having carried, not by identity
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Relay-retention tragedy-of-the-commons (#7 / §13.2) and per-neighbor Sybil cap bypass (#5)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Tit-for-tat WITHOUT identity, inspired by BitTorrent choke/unchoke and DTN encounter-record schemes (FDER) but stripped of any persistent ID. In each pairwise BLE session, a node offers buffer-acceptance proportional to what the PEER has demonstrably contributed IN THIS SESSION: 'I will accept up to k new blobs from you for every valid, not-yet-expired, not-already-seen blob you hand me that I find USEFUL (i.e., I didn't already have it).' A flooder handing you 10^6 junk-but-distinct blobs gains nothing because they're junk to you only if you can cheaply tell — so pair this with the have/want Bloom gossip already in §15: you advertise a Bloom of what you hold, the peer sends only blobs you LACK, and your acceptance budget for them is gated on the fraction of their offered blobs that were genuinely novel-and-valid. Sybil-rotating MACs doesn't help because the budget is per-SESSION and resets to baseline each session — a fresh 'neighbor' starts from a small floor, so churning identities caps you at the floor, not 15%-times-N. This is reciprocity over BEHAVIOR-in-the-moment, never over a remembered identity.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Gives generous-relaying an incentive gradient (you get carried in proportion to how much you carry) without any identity, addressing the spec's hardest open game-theory question. Caps Sybil buffer-capture at the per-session floor regardless of MAC churn.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Bends invariant #3 slightly: per-session behavioral budgets introduce a faint differentiation between a generous and a stingy neighbor, which is a (weak) metadata gradient. It also adds a 'usefulness' judgement that an attacker can game by mining distinct-but-valid blobs (the PoW gap). Net: raises the attacker's cost, doesn't eliminate. Soul-preserving fallback below.

constructiveAlternatives.title: SOUL-PRESERVING fallback for retention incentives: ambient-density-tied auto-knobs (no reciprocity, no identity, no gradient)
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Relay-retention tragedy-of-the-commons (#7) — the version that keeps invariant #3 perfectly intact
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Instead of behavioral reciprocity (which bends #3), make retention a pure function of LOCALLY-observed density, identical for every honest node and independent of any peer's behavior. Each phone estimates local node-density from the rate of distinct fresh IDs it sees (no identities, just a counter). When density is HIGH (many carriers, the commons is healthy), the node can safely lower its own size-cap — redundancy is plentiful, so being stingy is locally rational AND globally safe. When density is LOW (near the percolation cliff), the node automatically raises retention to the max its storage allows, because now every copy matters. This makes the GLOBALLY optimal behavior (carry more when scarce, less when abundant) the DEFAULT and automatic, eliminating the user's battery-vs-altruism conflict entirely — the tragedy-of-the-commons defection is removed by making the cooperative setting also the self-interested one in the regime where it matters. No peer behavior is observed, no per-neighbor differentiation exists, every node of equal density behaves identically.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Solves the commons problem with zero new metadata and zero identity — the cooperative default is automatic and density-aware. Pairs naturally with §10's density-aware UX.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Density estimate is gameable: an attacker emitting many distinct fresh IDs makes honest nodes BELIEVE density is high → they lower retention → mesh-carrying collapses (a flood that causes UNDER-retention). Must base the density estimate on hard-to-forge signals (e.g., count distinct PEER radios via PHY, not distinct blobs) and clamp the auto-lowering. Less powerful incentive than reciprocity (no reward for the generous), but perfectly soul-preserving.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Staggered / jittered panic-wipe to dissolve the mass-drop fingerprint
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Duress-wipe deanonymizing tell (#6)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Instead of an instantaneous wipe-everything-and-go-dark, the panic action: (1) immediately makes all content cryptographically inaccessible to the user/seizer (drop keys / overwrite at-rest keyslot — the SECURITY goal is met in milliseconds), but (2) lets the phone CONTINUE re-broadcasting the soup it was carrying for a randomized fade-out period, identical to how it would behave if those blobs were simply aging out naturally, AND (3) keeps emitting the cover-traffic floor throughout. From the persistent sensor net's view there is no synchronized mass-drop event — the radio's emission profile decays like any phone leaving the area / blobs hitting TTL. The seen-record insertion of wiped IDs (anti-reseed) is done lazily/locally and never needs to be visible. The duress-rotate's new identity is introduced gradually under cover, not as a clean cut-over. The key insight: the USER's security need (instant local inaccessibility) and the ANONYMITY need (no loud RF event) are separable — only the former must be instantaneous.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Removes the loudest-possible RF signature from the moment the user can least afford it, while keeping the local wipe instant. Turns panic into something indistinguishable from a phone walking away.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Brief window where the phone still relays others' (not its own secret) soup post-panic — acceptable since it can't read it and it's already public soup. Slightly more complex panic state machine; must guarantee the LOCAL key-destruction is genuinely instant and not gated on the fade-out.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Energy-aware TWO-floor uniformity to defuse the time-cap partition attack
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Time-cap low-power knob abuse (#7 partition lever)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Decouple as the spec already does (cover floor is independent of retention) but go further: define a minimum RELAY floor (not just a cover-EMISSION floor) that holds on any battery above a critical threshold — i.e., even 'barely participate / low-power' mode still carries SOME strangers' soup, not zero. The size of that relay floor scales with locally-observed density (cross-referencing the soul-preserving alt above): in a dense gathering the per-phone relay floor can be small because there are many phones; in a sparse mesh the floor rises because the network needs you. A phone only drops to literal zero relaying when truly critically low on battery, and that state is itself part of the uniform profile (everyone's phone does it at the same battery level, so it's not a per-user tell). This makes 'aggressive time-cap = mesh collapse' impossible to reach through the normal knob: the knob can reduce relaying toward the density-aware floor but not below it.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Prevents both accidental (battery-anxiety default) and adversarial knob-driven partition; ties carrying capacity to actual need.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Reduces user agency over their own battery (a hard relay floor costs power the user may resent); must be honest in UX. The density estimate it relies on is itself gameable (see #6 alt cost).

creativeIdeas: Treat TIME as just one of several independent 'aging clocks' a blob is subjected to, and let it die when ANY clock fires (logical AND of survival = OR of death): (a) absolute signed wall-TTL, (b) hop-energy decrement, (c) per-node real-elapsed-age FIFO under pressure, (d) gossip-median sanity bound. Each is weak alone and attacker-gameable in a different direction; their conjunction has no single lever. Frame the whole §6 as 'defense-in-depth on the death guarantee,' since the spec already treats death as the headline guarantee but currently rests it on one (clock) leg.

Make cover blobs DOUBLE as a distributed clock beacon: a fraction of cover traffic carries (signed-to-null) honest creation-timestamps whose only purpose is to feed neighbors' gossip-median clock. Cover traffic is mandatory anyway (invariant #3) and currently 'wasted' as pure noise — recycling it as a trust-minimized time source costs nothing extra on the wire and is perfectly uniform.

Reframe the seen-record's job: it doesn't need to remember 'every ID forever for 30 days,' it needs to remember 'every ID at least until it's certainly dead.' Tie seen-record retention-per-ID to that ID's OWN signed TTL (short-TTL messages can be forgotten from the seen-record sooner, since they can't legitimately resurrect after their own TTL+margin). This makes the seen-record cost scale with the SAME knob the sender already controls, turning short-TTL into a memory saving rather than the current flat 30-day cost — and it shrinks the attacker's memory-DoS surface for short-TTL floods.

Adversarial self-test the eviction policy as a CI gate: model eviction as a game where the attacker picks blob (TTL, arrival-time, neighbor-id) tuples to maximize P(target honest blob evicted before its honest delivery window), and require the chosen policy to keep that probability below a bound for a fixed attacker budget. This operationalizes open question §13.6 into a regression test rather than a one-off proof.

Expose 'mesh health' (estimated density, your effective remembering-window, your buffer pressure) as ambient UX — not as a knob but as weather. A user who can SEE that the mesh is sparse will naturally keep their phone in their pocket relaying, aligning human behavior with the percolation need without any enforced floor. This turns the tragedy-of-the-commons into a tragedy-of-the-VISIBLE-commons, which empirically defects far less.

Consider making panic-wipe and natural-death byte-for-byte identical in RF behavior as a DESIGN INVARIANT of its own: 'a phone leaving, a phone dying, a phone wiping, and a phone walking away must be indistinguishable.' This generalizes the staggered-wipe idea into a clean rule that the whole storage layer can be tested against.

techniques.name: Sliding-window / aging Bloom filters: A2 buffering (Yoon, 'Aging Bloom Filter with Two Active Buffers'), and Sliding-window Bloom via cuckoo+timestamp (SWBF; Liu et al.) — see survey arXiv:2106.12189 and arXiv:1712.01779 'Pay for a Sliding Bloom Filter and Get Counting/Distinct Elements for Free'
techniques.relevance: Direct replacement for the monolithic 30-day Bloom: hard-bounded memory, FIFO-by-time aging instead of FP-saturation, optional deletion — fixes seen-record DoS/FP attacks (#2,#3) and open question §13.8.

techniques.name: Cuckoo filter (Fan, Andersen, Kaminsky, Mitzenmacher, CoNEXT 2014, 'Cuckoo Filter: Practically Better Than Bloom')
techniques.relevance: Supports deletion (exact panic-purge of specific IDs) and lower space than counting Bloom at the FP rates needed here; backbone for a deletable seen-record.

techniques.name: Rate-Limiting Nullifiers (RLN), built on Semaphore; WAKU-RLN-RELAY (Vac/Logos Research)
techniques.relevance: The closest real anonymous per-epoch rate-limit primitive (one valid proof per epoch, Shamir-reveal on double-spend) — relevant to bounding flood volume that drives the seen-record/eviction attacks, though it assumes a group-membership setup that an OOB-only mesh would have to bootstrap; mark as non-trivial to port to a no-infrastructure DTN.

techniques.name: sdwdate (Whonix secure distributed-web date) and the SoK on digital-clock trustworthiness (arXiv:2502.09837); Threshold Logical Clocks (Ford, arXiv:1907.07010)
techniques.relevance: Prior art for trust-minimized time WITHOUT NTP — basis for the gossip-median mesh-epoch clock that gives the TTL death-guarantee a foundation in an offline mesh (attack #1).

techniques.name: Verifiable Delay Functions / Proof of Sequential Work (Boneh et al.; Wesolowski eprint 2018/623; non-interactive VDF timestamping eprint 2019/197)
techniques.relevance: A VDF chained from a blob's creation could give a clock-independent 'at least T elapsed' proof, an alternative path to TTL that doesn't trust wall-clocks — but verification cost on a weak phone and the lack of a shared start beacon make it a heavier option than hop-energy; mark speculative for this setting.

techniques.name: DTN flooding-attack defenses: Flooding Detection via Encounter Records (FDER, IEEE TIFS) and size-aware / utility-based buffer drop policies (SAD; Combined-Utility Router; MaxProp)
techniques.relevance: Closest DTN literature on buffer-drop under flood and on detecting flood via behavior — informs the per-session reciprocity and youngest-by-real-age eviction alternatives; note all canonical versions assume node identity, which must be stripped to fit invariants #4/#6.

techniques.name: BitTorrent choke/unchoke tit-for-tat (Cohen, 2003)
techniques.relevance: Model for in-the-moment, identity-free reciprocity (reward current behavior, forget the past each session) underpinning the proof-of-storage-time retention incentive (alt #5) and its Sybil resistance via per-session reset.

analyses.dimension: TRANSPORT — BLE flooding at gathering scale; iOS background BLE; mesh-layer hardening; airtime/connection DoS; battery; cover-traffic realism on the radio
analyses.assessment: The spec is unusually honest about its own ceilings (the §11 scale math, the iOS caveat in §12.5, "good crypto is not enough" in §10), and that honesty is its main strength. But the transport layer is where this design is most likely to simply not run, and the spec under-quantifies three physics walls that are more binding than the storage wall it spends a whole section on. (1) AIRTIME, not storage, is the real ceiling at gathering scale. The §11 table says 250k–2M live blobs "fit" a 1–2GB buffer — true for storage, but pushing even a fraction of that soup over BLE in a dense crowd is impossible. BLE has 3 advertising channels with NO frequency hopping; at ~200 co-located advertisers and 200ms intervals, measured collision probability is ~0.51, and discovery latency/energy rise roughly exponentially with density. A ~1KB blob is ~5–7 chained extended-advertising PDUs or one GATT connection (hundreds of ms setup, and Android caps ~7 concurrent connections with per-connection throughput collapsing as you add links). The mesh is airtime-starved long before it is storage-starved; the working set that can actually circulate in a stadium is orders of magnitude below 250k/hour. (2) iOS is not "best-effort," it is largely BROKEN for this model. Backgrounded iOS peripherals drop the local name, shove all service UUIDs into a 128-bit "overflow area" bitmask discoverable ONLY by explicitly-scanning iOS devices, slow advertising cadence, and ignore scan dedup — a backgrounded iPhone cannot emit a generic ID-free 1KB soup blob to Android at all, and barely to other foregrounded iPhones. This silently breaks Invariant 3 (uniformity) on iOS: an iPhone's emission profile is platform-distinguishable and mostly silent. (3) The anonymity model leans on "constant, ID-free BLE advertisement" and demoted MAC randomization, but PHY-layer fingerprinting (CFO/IQ radiometrics) hits ~100% device ID in under a minute among hundreds of devices — the spec acknowledges this in the threat table but its only listed defense (MAC rotation + payload-rotation sync) does nothing against it; the real defense (active CFO obfuscation, Oakland'24) is not in the design. None of these break the SOUL — flooding, uniformity, sealed-sender, offline-first are all still the right calls — but the transport plan needs a serious rework to be buildable, and several of the fixes below actually strengthen the invariants rather than bending them.

attacks.title: Airtime exhaustion is the true scale wall, mis-located as a storage wall
attacks.mechanism: §11 frames scale as a storage problem (count of blobs vs 1–2GB buffer). The binding constraint is airtime. BLE has only 3 advertising channels and no frequency hopping on them; published measurements show ~0.51 collision probability at 200 advertisers/200ms, with discovery latency and energy rising near-exponentially with device count. To gossip a ~1KB blob you either chain ~5–7 extended-adv PDUs (255B each) or open a GATT connection (hundreds of ms setup, Android ~7-connection cap, throughput per link collapsing under concurrency). In a 50k-person stadium even a tiny fraction simultaneously advertising saturates the 3 adv channels. The soup that can physically circulate per unit time is far smaller than the 250k–2M 'live blobs' the storage math blesses, so 'your friend's blob survives in everyone's buffer → delivery works' does not follow.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 7 (gathering-scale must actually work) and the §11 delivery claim; indirectly Invariant 3 (if you throttle to survive contention, real vs cover ratios shift).

attacks.title: iOS background silently violates uniformity and reach
attacks.mechanism: Backgrounded iOS: no local name advertised, all service UUIDs forced into a 128-bit overflow-area bitmask discoverable ONLY by explicitly-scanning iOS centrals, reduced advertising frequency, scan-dedup ignored, scan interval grows. A backgrounded iPhone literally cannot broadcast a generic ID-free 1KB soup blob that an Android device (or a non-scanning iPhone) will pick up. So an iPhone's wire signature is platform-distinguishable from an Android's, and is near-silent in background. The §10 'charge-independent uniformity floor' and §1 'output looks the same whether or not you're sending' are false on iOS background. An adversary trivially partitions the crowd into iOS-background (silent), iOS-foreground (chatty), Android, which is metadata.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 3 (uniformity / indistinguishability), Invariant 1 (full function without internet — half the phones can't relay).

attacks.title: PHY-layer radiometric fingerprinting nullifies the wire-anonymity stack
attacks.mechanism: The design demotes MAC randomization and relies on 'no stable advertising tokens' + payload-rotation-synced-to-MAC-rotation. None of that touches the radio. CFO/IQ-imbalance radiometric fingerprinting (Oakland'22, Oakland'24) identifies a specific handset with ~100% accuracy in <1 minute among hundreds of devices. The threat table concedes the SDR adversary 'links your packets to your handset' and argues it only yields 'you emit blobs' — but combined with the active-delete signal (§7/§12.3), persistent presence/absence at a location, and the iOS-vs-Android partition above, per-handset linkage is a strong base for statistical disclosure and co-presence graphs over a multi-day gathering.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 4/3 robustness (the 'you only learn they emit blobs' claim weakens to 'I track THIS handset's emission timeline and location').

attacks.title: Connection/airtime flood DoS is cheaper than blob flood and harder to evict
attacks.mechanism: §8 link-layer defenses assume the cost is in receiving full blobs and defend with PoW-prefix exchange + per-neighbor caps. But an attacker doesn't need valid blobs: spamming connectable advertisements / GATT connection requests, or just flooding the 3 advertising channels with garbage at line rate, denies discovery and connection setup to everyone in radio range. The defender (weakest battery phone) must still listen and arbitrate. Per-neighbor caps don't help when the attacker mints unlimited ephemeral neighbor identities (no identity on the wire — by design). This is a direct consequence of Invariant 2/4 (no identity, no targeting): you cannot rate-limit a source you refuse to name.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 1/7 (mesh stops functioning under a cheap local jam-by-protocol attack).

attacks.title: Cover-traffic realism is far more expensive on the radio than the spec assumes
attacks.mechanism: §10 wants dummies that 'propagate exactly like real messages' and 'sample the bursty/diurnal human send distribution.' Propagating a dummy means it consumes the same scarce airtime and the same relay buffers across the whole component as a real message. At gathering density (already airtime-starved per attack #1), a realistic real:dummy ratio (say 1:5 to 1:20 for a usable anonymity set) multiplies the airtime crisis 5–20x. Worse: a too-cheap or too-regular dummy generator becomes its own discriminator (constant-rate cover is itself a tell, which the spec notes — but then must pay for bursty cover too). Cover traffic and airtime scarcity are in direct conflict and the spec budgets neither.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 3 (if you cut cover to save airtime, uniformity drops; if you keep cover, capacity craters).

attacks.title: Trial-decrypt + chained-fragment reassembly is a CPU/battery DoS at volume
attacks.mechanism: Every new blob → one trial-decrypt (XChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD attempt) plus, with one-time prekeys, potentially multiple candidate-key attempts. Open question §13.5 flags this. On the radio side it compounds: chained extended-adv blobs must be reassembled from 5–7 fragments before you even know if it's well-formed; an attacker floods half-blobs / mismatched fragments to make you buffer-and-reassemble garbage, then fail. The 'PoW-prefix before full transfer' gate helps for connections but not for connectionless advertising fragments, which have no handshake.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 1/7 (battery-weakest node falls over; uniformity floor can't be sustained when CPU-saturated).

attacks.title: 'Constant emission' assumption fights every OS power governor
attacks.mechanism: §10's 'charge-independent uniformity floor' and §1's continuous re-broadcast require sustained background BLE TX/scan. Android Doze, app-standby buckets, OEM battery killers (the well-documented Chinese-OEM background-kill problem), and iOS background suspension all throttle or kill exactly this. So real-world emission is governed by each phone's OS power state and OEM, which is (a) not constant and (b) correlated with phone model and charge state — i.e., the uniformity floor is actually a per-device, per-OEM variable that an observer can profile.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 3 (uniformity becomes device/OS-dependent), Invariant 1 (reach depends on which OEMs are in the crowd).

constructiveAlternatives.title: Gossip via SET RECONCILIATION (IBLT/minisketch over message-IDs), not blob-by-blob flooding
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Airtime exhaustion (attack #1); trial-decrypt/fragment DoS (#6)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Keep pure flooding semantics, but change the WIRE protocol from 'rebroadcast every blob' to 'rebroadcast a compact sketch of which message-IDs I hold, then transfer only the symmetric difference.' Two neighbors exchange a minisketch/IBLT of their 256-bit IDs (minisketch is bandwidth-optimal for small diffs, ~d*64 bits for d differences; IBLT for large diffs), decode the diff, and transfer only blobs the other lacks. This collapses redundant airtime from O(every blob * every contact) to O(new blobs only). It is still undirected (you reconcile with WHOEVER you meet, no destination), still no path metadata — the sketch reveals only a set of opaque content-addressed IDs you already openly carry. Cover blobs are real members of the set, so they reconcile identically.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Order-of-magnitude airtime reduction; this is the single highest-leverage transport change and is proven in production (Bitcoin Erlay/minisketch). Makes gathering-scale physically plausible instead of just storage-plausible.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: More CPU (sketch decode), more protocol complexity, and the ID-set sketch is a (content-addressed, non-routing) metadata surface to analyze for k-anonymity. Need to bound decode failure (Bloom/IBLT listing-failure probability, see §13.8).

constructiveAlternatives.title: Connectionless-first transport: chained extended advertising as the default carrier, connections only as fallback
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Airtime exhaustion (#1); connection-flood DoS (#4); iOS background (#2, partially)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Make the primary transport BLE 5 extended/periodic advertising (connectionless broadcast, up to ~1650B chained — enough for a ~1KB blob) rather than GATT connections. Connectionless broadcast is inherently one-to-many (perfect for flooding), needs no per-pair setup (kills the connection-setup-DoS and the Android 7-connection cap), and is the natural 'soup' medium. Use BLE Periodic Advertising with PAwR (Periodic Advertising with Responses, BT 5.4) where available so a scanner can pull a wanted blob without a full connection. Reserve GATT connections only for bulk catch-up between two long-dwell neighbors.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Matches the flooding model to the radio primitive built for it; removes the connection-setup tax and the connection-flood attack surface; one advertised blob is heard by all neighbors at once.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Extended/periodic advertising support varies by chipset and Android version; iOS exposes almost none of this to apps (so iOS still needs the bridge approach below). Throughput per advertiser is still bounded by the 3 adv channels — must combine with set reconciliation (#1) to control how OFTEN you broadcast.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Wi-Fi Aware (NAN) as a co-located high-throughput carrier, BLE as the always-on discovery/uniformity floor
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Airtime exhaustion (#1); iOS background break (#2); cover-traffic cost (#5)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Dual-radio: BLE remains the constant, low-power, ID-free presence/uniformity beacon and bootstrap; once two phones are co-located, hand the bulk soup transfer to Wi-Fi Aware / NAN, which runs at Wi-Fi speeds, is purpose-built for crowded co-located clusters, needs no AP/internet, and as of iOS 26 + Android 8 is cross-platform. NAN clusters form exactly the dense-gathering topology you target. Keep the SAME uniform-soup semantics on top (fixed blobs, trial-decrypt, no routing) — only the link layer changes. Cover traffic becomes cheap because NAN has bandwidth headroom BLE never will.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: 10–100x the throughput of BLE mesh; makes realistic cover ratios affordable; turns the airtime wall into a much higher Wi-Fi wall; finally gives iOS a real foreground relay path. Directly raises the feasible gathering scale.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Bends Invariant 1's spirit only at the EDGE: NAN is higher power and its co-presence/cluster discovery is a richer metadata surface than ID-free BLE (NAN MitM/desync attacks exist in the literature — must threat-model). It is still fully offline (no AP/internet), so the letter of Invariant 1 holds. SOUL-PRESERVING fallback is mandatory: the system MUST run BLE-only when NAN is unavailable (older devices, background), so NAN is opportunistic acceleration, never required — exactly mirroring the internet-bridge philosophy in §9.

constructiveAlternatives.title: iOS reality: explicit dual-role scanning + a documented 'iOS relays best in foreground / when charging' UX contract
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: iOS background break (#2); OS power governors (#7)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Accept the platform and engineer to it instead of around it. (a) On iOS, every app instance runs BOTH central and peripheral roles and ALWAYS scans (background scan with a service-UUID filter is the only way to read another iPhone's overflow-area advertisement). (b) Use Core Bluetooth state preservation/restoration so the app relaunches into BLE events. (c) Build the iOS↔Android bridge through devices that ARE foregrounded/charging (a few 'anchor' phones on chargers at a protest, or a cheap dedicated Android/ESP32 'soup brick'). (d) Make the UX contract explicit and honest: 'iPhone in pocket = listening but barely relaying; keep the app open or plug in to help the mesh.' This converts a silent invariant violation into a visible, user-understood degradation.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Honest, shippable iOS behavior; preserves reach by routing relay duty to capable devices; matches what BitChat/Briar learned in the field (Android-first, density-dependent).
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Bends Invariant 3 on iOS background (iPhone emission is admittedly weaker/different) — but this is ALREADY true and currently undocumented; making it explicit is strictly better. SOUL-PRESERVING companion: keep a uniformity FLOOR via foreground/charging and avoid emitting anything that distinguishes a real-sending iPhone from a relaying one within the role it CAN sustain.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Active CFO/PHY obfuscation + emission-time jitter to blunt radiometric fingerprinting
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: PHY fingerprinting (#3)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: On chipsets that expose it, apply the Oakland'24 'practical BLE PHY-layer fingerprint obfuscation' (randomized carrier-frequency-offset injection) so the handset's CFO is not a stable identifier — the paper shows attacker accuracy dropping to ~10–20% over random after 24h vs ~100% in 1 minute unobfuscated. Where the radio is closed, approximate at the protocol layer: randomize TX power, advertising cadence, and channel selection per emission, and add per-emission timing jitter so presence/absence and inter-packet timing don't form a clean per-device timeline. Pair with the existing MAC+payload rotation so all observable layers rotate together.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Closes the one threat the spec concedes but does not defend; upgrades 'they can track your handset' back toward 'they see undifferentiated soup.' Strengthens Invariants 3 and 4 against the SDR adversary.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: CFO obfuscation needs chipset/driver access most phones don't expose today (likely a future-hardware or rooted-device feature); protocol-layer approximations are weaker. Timing jitter slightly reduces effective airtime. Mark hardware availability as uncertain.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Airtime-fair MAC discipline: token-bucket TX budget + adaptive cadence keyed to local density
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Connection/airtime flood DoS (#4); airtime exhaustion (#1)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Give each device a self-imposed airtime token bucket: it measures local channel occupancy (it's already scanning) and adapts its own advertising interval up as density rises (CSMA-like backoff for the 3 adv channels; published backoff schemes for crowded BLE exist). For DoS: rank NEIGHBORS by observed channel-time consumed (a link-local, identity-free signal — you can measure 'this radio is hogging airtime' without naming it) and de-prioritize listening to the hoggers, while keeping the uniformity floor. This is link-local and identity-free, so it doesn't reintroduce per-source identity.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Graceful degradation under both honest congestion and malicious airtime floods; keeps the weakest-battery defender from being the arbiter of unbounded traffic. Uses only locally-observable radio behavior.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Adaptive cadence interacts with uniformity (cadence becomes density-dependent — but density is public, so it's not a per-device tell if everyone uses the same rule). An adversary cycling fresh ephemeral radios can still consume airtime; this caps damage, doesn't eliminate it (the real fix is the unsolved anonymous rate-limiting, §13.1).

constructiveAlternatives.title: Density-adaptive blob sizing / fragmentation budget to fit the airtime envelope
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Airtime exhaustion (#1); cover-traffic cost (#5)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Keep the 255-char/fixed-size uniformity for the SEALED payload, but make the GOSSIP cadence and how many blobs you actively push per window a function of measured local airtime, not a constant. In a packed stadium you reconcile less aggressively (rely on the huge number of carriers to eventually move every blob); in a sparse area you push harder. Couple this with the §6 'shorter TTL stretches density' lever as an explicit airtime knob, not just a storage knob.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Lets the system survive the airtime wall by trading delivery latency (acceptable — it's a delay-tolerant 'message in a bottle') for capacity, instead of failing hard. Blob size stays uniform.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Higher delivery latency in dense settings; needs careful proof that a specific blob still percolates before TTL under reduced cadence (this is the percolation question §13, now airtime-bounded not just topology-bounded).

creativeIdeas: Reframe the headline math: ship a §11-companion 'AIRTIME budget' table (live blobs that can CIRCULATE per minute given 3 adv channels, collision probability vs density, and chosen cadence) alongside the storage table. Right now the spec proves the buffer fits the soup but not that the radio can stir it. The airtime table will likely set the real scope (and may pleasantly show set-reconciliation buys back an order of magnitude).

Treat the soup as content-addressed gossip and borrow Bitcoin Erlay wholesale: minisketch reconciliation of message-IDs over short-lived encounters is almost exactly the 'sync an insert-only set across flaky peers, minimize bandwidth' problem it was built for. The 256-bit message-ID = hash(secret) is already a perfect set element.

Embrace dedicated 'soup bricks': cheap always-charging ESP32/Android relays that protest organizers scatter (taped under benches, in backpacks). They have no identity, no keys, just carry+rebroadcast — pure relays. They fix the iOS-can't-relay-in-background problem AND the percolation cliff in sparse areas, while preserving every invariant (they're just more uniform-soup nodes). Field-realistic and cheap.

Use BLE Periodic Advertising with Responses (PAwR, BT 5.4): a scanner can request a specific advertised blob in response slots WITHOUT a full connection — a connectionless 'I want that one' that maps cleanly onto have/want gossip and dodges the connection-flood DoS. Worth a hardware-availability spike.

Make cover traffic do double duty as airtime probing: dummies can carry (inside the seal, invisible to all) the emitter's locally-measured channel-occupancy estimate, letting neighbors crowd-source a density map for adaptive cadence — turning unavoidable cover into a useful, still-uniform signal.

Steal the BitChat field lesson explicitly: it shipped a 7-hop TTL, ~300m relay, real protest use in Madagascar/Nepal in 2025 — and broke instantly at the identity layer, not the radio. Validates flooding+density and screams 'spend your hardening budget on the mesh/identity layer,' exactly where §10 already points. Cite it as a live data point that the soul (offline BLE flooding for gatherings) WORKS and the danger is the seams.

Add a 'transport conformance' test like §9's internet-disabled CI: a multi-OS field harness (N Android OEMs + iOS foreground/background + a soup brick) that MEASURES real circulation rate, iOS emission gaps, and per-device airtime, so the uniformity-floor and reach claims are continuously verified on real silicon, not assumed.

Consider audio/ultrasound or QR-burst as an emergency zero-radio side channel for the OOB key exchange and for tiny high-value blobs when BLE is jammed — keeps the 'works in a total blackout AND under active RF jamming' promise honest without adding routing.

techniques.name: Minisketch / Erlay (Bitcoin set reconciliation, Wuille et al.)
techniques.relevance: Bandwidth-optimal reconciliation of large opaque ID sets over flaky peers — directly applicable to gossiping which message-IDs each node holds; collapses flooding redundancy. Production-proven.

techniques.name: Invertible Bloom Lookup Tables (IBLT) / Rateless IBLT
techniques.relevance: Set-difference recovery when diffs are large; complements minisketch (efficient for small diffs). Use to choose blobs to transfer; mind listing-failure probability (ties to spec §13.8 Bloom sizing).

techniques.name: BLE 5 Extended & Periodic Advertising; PAwR (BT 5.4)
techniques.relevance: Connectionless one-to-many carriers matching the flooding model; ~255B per PDU / ~1650B chained fits a ~1KB blob; PAwR gives request/response without connections. Chipset/OS availability varies — verify on target hardware.

techniques.name: Wi-Fi Aware / Neighbor Awareness Networking (NAN)
techniques.relevance: Offline, AP-less, crowd-optimized high-throughput co-located data path; iOS 26 + Android 8+ cross-platform as of 2025. Candidate opportunistic accelerator under BLE. Note OpenNAN desync/MitM attack literature when threat-modeling.

techniques.name: Practical Obfuscation of BLE Physical-Layer Fingerprints (Givehchian et al., IEEE S&P/Oakland 2024)
techniques.relevance: Active CFO randomization defense that drops radiometric-fingerprinting accuracy from ~100%/1min to ~10–20% over random/24h — the missing defense for the SDR/PHY adversary the spec concedes.

techniques.name: Evaluating Physical-Layer BLE Location Tracking Attacks (Givehchian et al., Oakland 2022)
techniques.relevance: Quantifies the radiometric-fingerprinting threat (~unique ID in <1min among hundreds) that the spec's MAC/payload-rotation does not address.

techniques.name: BLE advertising collision models & crowded-BLE backoff schemes (e.g., Kim 2017 IET Comms; MDPI Sensors collision studies)
techniques.relevance: Empirical collision probability vs device count/interval (~0.51 at 200 devices/200ms) and CSMA-like backoff for the 3 adv channels — the basis for the airtime budget and adaptive-cadence MAC discipline.

techniques.name: Core Bluetooth background processing constraints + overflow-area reverse-engineering (David G. Young)
techniques.relevance: Documents iOS background peripheral behavior (no local name, 128-bit overflow bitmask, reduced cadence, scan-dedup ignored) — the concrete basis for the iOS dual-role/anchor-device strategy.

techniques.name: Briar / Bramble (BSP, BTP) and BitChat (2025) field deployments
techniques.relevance: Real-world DTN-over-Bluetooth precedent: confirm density dependence, ~10–30m range, multi-hop fragility, and that failures cluster at the identity/mesh seam, not the radio — guides where to spend hardening effort. BitChat protest data (Madagascar/Nepal 2025) is a live soul-works datapoint.

analyses.dimension: THREAT-UX — Threat-model honesty + UX/operational security (OOB key-exchange usability vs MITM, panic/duress ergonomics, false beliefs, plausible deniability, the bootstrap/cold-start problem, marketing-vs-reality gap)
analyses.assessment: The spec is unusually honest at the engineering layer (§12 limitations, the "good crypto is not enough" framing, killing the public tombstone) and that honesty is its biggest UX asset. But honesty in a design doc is not honesty in a product. The dangerous gap is between what the doc admits and what a user holding the running app will believe. Three structural UX problems are under-treated. (1) OOB verification is treated as a solved invariant ("forecloses the Bridgefy MITM class") — but the empirical record is brutal: only ~13-14% of users complete the Signal authentication ceremony correctly, and the meldingx design actually moves MORE weight onto that ceremony than Signal does, because there is no server, no key-transparency, no out-of-band recovery, and rotation produces silent unreadability rather than a visible "safety number changed" warning. The MITM is foreclosed only if the QR scan is observed and uncorrupted; the doc never specifies what the user sees, what a mismatch looks like, or how a relationship survives rotation. (2) The product's entire emotional promise — "self-destructing message in a bottle," "leave no durable trace" — is exactly the promise that disappearing-message research shows users systematically over-trust. The default (local-purge + TTL) does NOT delete the recipient's copy, screenshots, OS notification mirrors, keyboard/clipboard caches, or seized retained ciphertext, yet the marketing one-liner invites the belief that it does. This is the bitchat failure mode (Dorsey shipped "not robust" while floating Kenya protests). (3) The bootstrap problem is acknowledged as "open question (h)" framed as percolation physics, but it is really a UX/go-to-market death spiral: zero density at launch means zero delivery means uninstall, and the cover-traffic floor means the app drains battery 24/7 while delivering nothing — the worst possible first-run experience. None of these break a crypto invariant; all of them break the product, and the gathering-scope invariant makes the bootstrap problem unusually acute because you need a *crowd* on day one, in one place, simultaneously. The good news: every one of these has soul-preserving fixes, and most are about honest UX framing and pairing-time ceremony design, not architecture.

attacks.title: OOB QR exchange is a supervised-MITM / shoulder-relay, not just a remote MITM
attacks.mechanism: The doc claims OOB exchange 'forecloses the Bridgefy MITM-over-the-air class.' True for a remote network attacker. But the real-world OOB ceremony for a protest/blackout crowd is two strangers scanning each other in a tense, crowded, low-trust environment. An adversary who controls one endpoint (an infiltrator, a borrowed/seized phone, a compromised display) can present a key the victim cannot distinguish from a legitimate one — there is no out-of-band channel ABOVE the QR to verify the QR (the QR *is* the channel). Worse, with no server and no key transparency (Trail of Bits' exact criticism of bitchat: 'serverless architecture cannot deploy key transparency'), there is no later detection of a fake-key attack. The empirical completion rate for fingerprint/safety-number ceremonies is 13-14%; meldingx leans on it harder than Signal does and offers no fallback. The 'foreclosed' claim is overstated and will become a CVE-class finding the moment a researcher tries it.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 4 (sealed-sender: sender/recipient hidden from everyone but each other). A successful pairing-time MITM means a third party is now 'each other,' silently and permanently, with no detection path.

attacks.title: Key rotation is a silent failure that users will read as 'they ghosted me' or 'app is broken'
attacks.mechanism: Rotation = new keypair; 'old ID becomes unreadable to you.' Signal at least shows 'safety number changed.' Here, after a contact rotates (or is compromised and rotates maliciously, or a revocation note hasn't propagated in a blackout), your messages to them silently vanish into undecryptable soup and theirs to you trial-decrypt to nothing. The user has no signal distinguishing (a) friend rotated keys, (b) friend out of range, (c) MITM swapped keys, (d) message died at TTL, (e) app bug. In a high-stakes setting this ambiguity is dangerous: a person may believe a message was delivered/safe when it was silently dropped, or believe a contact is compromised when they merely rotated. The revocation note 'propagates as an ordinary message' — but in the blackout/sparse regime (your hardest case, §12.2) it may never arrive, so the security-critical 'this key is dead' signal is delivered over the least-reliable channel exactly when it matters most.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 6 (no server) interacts badly here — without any rendezvous there is no way to learn a rotation out-of-band except meeting again in person. The death-guarantee promise (Invariant 5) is also undermined: a user can falsely believe they sent a self-destructing message that actually went nowhere.

attacks.title: The product promise teaches a false belief the architecture cannot honor
attacks.mechanism: One-liner: 'leave no durable trace' / 'message in a bottle.' Disappearing-message research is unanimous that users over-trust ephemerality and behave MORE recklessly because of it. The meldingx default deletes NOTHING on the recipient side beyond their voluntary local purge: screenshots, the OS notification shade (lock-screen preview persists after in-app deletion), clipboard, predictive-keyboard learning, accessibility-service loggers, and — critically — any node that retained the ciphertext can decrypt it later if it ever obtains a static key (§12.4 admits this). A user reading the marketing will believe 'we can both make this gone'; the truth is 'gone from cooperating buffers, never from a hostile recipient or a retained-ciphertext + key-seizure adversary.' This is the precise bitchat marketing-vs-reality failure that drew the Trail of Bits writeup. In a protest, this false belief gets people arrested.
attacks.breaks: Invariant 5 (self-destructing) at the level of user-perceived guarantee. The TTL is a real death guarantee for HONEST nodes only; the UX implies a universal one.

attacks.title: Cold-start death spiral, made worse by the always-on cover-traffic floor
attacks.mechanism: Gathering-scope (Invariant 7) requires a dense crowd to be useful. At launch there is no crowd. First-run experience: app installs, immediately begins emitting PoW-valid cover blobs 24/7 (charge-independent floor, §10), drains battery, and delivers exactly zero messages because no peers exist. This is the worst onboarding in messaging: cost up front, value never. FireChat's real usage was mostly LTE-backed; pure-mesh adoption has never bootstrapped. The density requirement and the no-internet invariant combine into a chicken-and-egg with no escape ramp, because the usual cold-start fix (a server-backed fallback so the app is useful with one user) is forbidden by Invariants 1 and 6. The cover-traffic floor is anonymity-correct but adoption-poison: it makes the empty-network state actively costly.
attacks.breaks: Bends the *viability* of Invariants 1, 3, 7 simultaneously — not a crypto break, a 'no one is on the network so the anonymity set is 1 and the app is uninstalled' break. Anonymity-via-uniformity (Invariant 3) is meaningless at low N; k-anonymity with k=2 is deanonymization.

attacks.title: Panic/duress ergonomics fail under real coercion and create a NEW tell
attacks.mechanism: The duress variant (wipe + rotate, return as new ID) assumes the coercer (a) doesn't watch you type, (b) doesn't demand you demonstrate sending a message to a known contact, and (c) can't tell a duress-wipe from a normal empty inbox. But the deeper duress-PIN literature (Random Oracle) shows the hard part is plausibility: if the coercer knows the app HAS a duress mode, an empty/rotated state is itself evidence you triggered it ('why is your contact list empty? unlock the real one'). meldingx's own seen-record makes this worse: after a panic delete-all, your seen-record now contains the IDs you just purged (anti-resurrection), which is a forensic artifact proving messages existed and were deleted — the opposite of deniability. A phone forensic tool dumping a fat Bloom filter right after a 'clean' inbox is a contradiction a coercer can exploit.
attacks.breaks: The plausible-deniability sub-goal implied by Invariant 5's duress variant. Also leaks against the device-seizure adversary in §3 row 7, which the doc rates only ⚠️.

attacks.title: Prekey exhaustion downgrade is a security cliff hidden behind a toast
attacks.mechanism: One-time prekeys give forward secrecy; on exhaustion the system silently falls back to the static key 'with a UX warning.' In the offline gathering case, prekeys exhaust FAST (no server to replenish; you can only top up in-person), and the busiest, highest-risk contacts exhaust first. The 'UX warning' is the same class of dialog users tap through 100% of the time. The user keeps messaging, now without forward secrecy, believing they still have it. A seized static key then decrypts the entire post-exhaustion history (§12.4). The warning is necessary but empirically useless as a control; the security property silently degrades for the most active relationships.
attacks.breaks: The forward-secrecy promise (§4/§12.4) at the perceived level; the user's mental model says 'FS on' while the wire says 'static fallback.'

attacks.title: Density-aware 'origination may be detectable' warning is unactionable and will be ignored or panic-inducing
attacks.mechanism: §10 promises a UX warning when cover is thin. But the user cannot DO anything actionable with 'origination may be detectable' except not send — which defeats the product in exactly the sparse/blackout regime ranked #1. Either the warning is so frequent it's banner-blindness, or it appears at the worst moment (you NEED to send, alone, in a sparse area) and either deters a legitimate critical message or is ignored. It also implicitly tells the user 'you are now identifiable as a sender,' which in a real threat scenario is terrifying and may cause worse behavior (turning the app off, which is itself a tell that correlates with 'about to do something').
attacks.breaks: Invariant 3 (anonymity via uniformity) at the human layer: the warning concedes that uniformity has failed in sparse conditions, and the user's only safe response (stop sending) defeats Invariant 1 (works with no internet / in a blackout), turning the two invariants against each other.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Mutual-presence pairing ceremony: bidirectional QR + spoken SAS + a shared physical secret
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: OOB QR supervised-MITM; low ceremony completion rate
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Replace 'scan a QR' with a 3-second ceremony that is hard to MITM in person and easy to complete: (1) both phones simultaneously display AND scan each other (mutual QR, both directions in one motion so neither side can present a substituted key without the other noticing a mismatch animation), (2) after the scan both phones derive a Short Authentication String from the combined transcript and show it as 3 emoji or 4 dictionary words; the two humans glance and confirm they match (verbal/visual SAS comparison is the highest-usability OOB form per the fingerprint-length and word-based-verification studies), (3) optionally bind a low-entropy shared secret typed identically on both phones (a word you both agree aloud) into the transcript, so even a perfect display-swap fails. The whole thing is a single 'hold phones together, check the 3 emoji match, tap' gesture. Crucially: NO key material is ever on the wire (still OOB), so all invariants hold. This raises completion from ~14% toward the 'glance-and-match' regime that word/emoji SAS studies show is far better than 60-digit comparison.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Turns the weakest-but-claimed-strong link into an actually-strong one; makes the 'forecloses MITM' claim defensible; matches the in-person, no-server model exactly.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: More pairing UX work; requires both phones present and cameras working (already the OOB assumption). Slightly longer than a one-way scan.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Honest, layered trust UI: 'Verified in person' vs 'Unverified' badges, and a loud rotation/MITM signal
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Silent rotation failure; false belief of confidentiality; missing key-change warning
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Borrow Signal's 'safety number changed' but make it impossible to ignore in a way that respects no-server: each contact carries a persistent local trust state {Verified-in-person, Unverified, Key-changed!}. Verified is set only by the mutual ceremony above. If a contact's key ever differs from the pinned one (rotation OR MITM — you can't tell which without re-meeting, and the UI must SAY that), the contact goes red 'Key changed — verify in person before trusting' and messages to/from them are quarantined behind a tap, never silently dropped. For the 'is it rotation, range, TTL, or bug?' ambiguity: give send-side states the user can actually reason about — 'Released to the soup' (NOT 'delivered'; you cannot know delivery without the optional ack), 'No peers nearby — holding,' 'Expired (TTL) before any peer.' This is pure local UI; nothing goes on the wire.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Closes the silent-failure gap; gives the user a correct mental model of an inherently best-effort, no-ACK system; converts a CVE-class silent MITM into a visible 'verify in person' prompt.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: UI complexity; risk of badge-blindness if overused (mitigate: only Verified vs not; reserve red strictly for key-change).

constructiveAlternatives.title: Truth-in-labeling: ship the limitations as first-run UX, not just a design doc §12
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Marketing-vs-reality gap; false belief of universal deletion; the bitchat failure mode
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Make the threat model a product surface, not buried prose. Concretely: (1) Drop 'leave no durable trace' from the top-line; replace with the honest, still-compelling 'messages expire from the network on a timer — but anyone you message can screenshot or keep their copy.' (2) A 30-second, skippable-but-default-on first-run 'What this does and does NOT protect' card set, written at a 6th-grade level: 'Hides WHO you message from bystanders ✔ / Cannot stop a screenshot ✘ / Useless if no one nearby has the app ✘ / Verify contacts in person or a stranger could read your messages ⚠.' (3) Per-feature inline honesty: the TTL picker says 'Your copy and cooperating phones delete this. The person you send to can keep it.' (4) A standing 'Reality' screen in settings restating §12. This is the single highest-leverage anti-harm intervention and costs only copywriting + a few screens.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Prevents the protest-arrest-from-false-confidence harm; pre-empts the inevitable 'security researcher dunks on the marketing' cycle; honesty is itself a differentiator and a trust-builder.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Marketing must swallow a less magical pitch; some onboarding friction. That friction is the point.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Bootstrap via 'rooms/swarms' and a useful single-user mode, without breaking offline/no-server
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Cold-start death spiral; empty-network battery drain with zero value
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Three soul-preserving moves. (a) Deliberate co-location seeding: ship a 'start a swarm here' first-run flow built for the gathering scope — a printable/sharable poster QR that just installs the app and pre-shares an *event-scoped contact set*, so the first time 50 people in a stadium open it they already have a populated contact list and the soup is non-empty immediately. This is OOB key exchange at crowd scale (one QR -> the event's published organizer keys), still no server, still in-person/physical. (b) Useful-when-alone mode: when zero peers are detected for N minutes, drop to a near-zero emission 'standby' (a tiny periodic beacon to detect density, NOT the full cover floor) and tell the user honestly 'No one nearby is using this yet — it will work when others are around.' This fixes the battery-for-nothing first impression while preserving the uniformity floor *whenever the network is actually live* (the floor only needs to hold when there's traffic to hide among — k-anonymity at N=0 is vacuous anyway). (c) Pair-to-activate: the app is genuinely useful for two people who paired in person even at N=2 (direct-range bottle), so the smallest viable network is a friend pair, not a crowd.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Breaks the chicken-and-egg by making density a thing you bring (your group) rather than a thing you wait for; eliminates the worst onboarding moment; gives a real reason to keep the app installed between gatherings.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Bends Invariant 3 slightly: the standby/near-zero mode means uniformity is not literally charge-independent-constant at N=0 (it's relaxed when there is provably no one to hide among). Argue this is sound — uniformity's job is to hide a real sender in a crowd; with no crowd there is nothing to hide and the floor only burns battery. The risk is a transition tell (going from standby to active = 'someone arrived'); mitigate by ramping cover smoothly with observed density, not stepping.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Soul-preserving alternative to the bent invariant above: constant floor, but make standby the cover
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: The transition-tell risk introduced by the standby-mode bootstrap fix
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: If you refuse to bend Invariant 3 at all: keep a strictly constant emission profile from install, but solve the battery-for-nothing problem by making the FLOOR itself extremely cheap and duty-cycled identically in all states (e.g., a fixed 1%-duty BLE cadence that is the same whether N=0 or N=10000), and recoup adoption purely through honest framing ('this uses ~X% battery/day to stay ready') plus the rooms/poster seeding (move a) and pair-to-activate (move c) above, which don't touch the floor at all. The constant floor never reveals density transitions because it never changes; the cost is you accept a small permanent battery draw as the price of uniformity and tell the user so.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Keeps anonymity-via-uniformity literally intact; still fixes most of the cold-start problem via seeding and pair-mode.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Permanent background battery cost even when alone; a worse first impression than the standby option — you're trading adoption for a clean invariant.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Duress that is plausible by construction: hidden-volume inbox + believable decoy, and a deniable seen-record
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Panic/duress fails under a coercer who knows the feature exists; seen-record is a 'deletion happened' artifact
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Two parts. (1) Replace 'wipe + rotate' (which leaves a suspicious empty state) with a hidden-volume model from the deniable-storage literature (DataLair / TrueCrypt-style, adapted): the duress credential opens a fully-functional DECOY identity with plausible mundane contacts and recent benign messages (auto-aged so it looks lived-in), while the real identity is cryptographically indistinguishable from random padding in the encrypted-at-rest store. The coercer sees a working app with content, not a suspicious blank — which the duress-PIN literature identifies as the actual hard requirement ('the attacker cannot distinguish'). (2) Make the seen-record deniable: store it encrypted under the SAME at-rest scheme so a locked phone yields a featureless blob; and after panic, instead of dumping purged IDs in cleartext (a 'these existed and were deleted' tell), keep the anti-resurrection function but make the post-panic Bloom statistically indistinguishable from a normal-aged Bloom (pre-salt it / keep it always near-full of cover IDs so 'lots of entries' is the steady state, not a deletion signature).
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Turns duress from 'evidence you triggered duress' into genuine deniability; removes the forensic contradiction between an empty inbox and a fat seen-record.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Hidden-volume storage is complex and has its own multi-snapshot caveats; maintaining a convincing decoy identity is ongoing UX work; always-full cover Bloom costs some memory.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Replace tap-through security warnings with structural defaults (forward secrecy, density)
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Prekey-exhaustion downgrade ignored; density warning unactionable
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Stop relying on warnings users tap through. (1) Prekey exhaustion: instead of silently falling back to static-key and showing a toast, make the DEFAULT for an exhausted contact to QUEUE-AND-HOLD (don't send without FS) and surface a single clear action: 'You're out of forward-secret keys for Ana — re-pair in person to refresh' — i.e., make the secure path the path of least resistance and the insecure static fallback an explicit, deliberate opt-in ('send anyway without forward secrecy' behind a confirm), not the silent default. (2) Density: replace the abstract 'origination may be detectable' with a concrete, calm capacity meter ('~3 phones nearby — low cover') shown BEFORE composing, so the user makes the risk decision while choosing whether to send at all, not via a post-hoc scare banner. Frame it as the bottle metaphor: 'few bottles in the water right now.'
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Converts two ignored-warning failures into structural choices; keeps the user's mental model aligned with the wire reality.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Hold-and-queue can delay messages (acceptable for a best-effort system; must be clearly shown); requires real-time peer-count estimation, which is cheap from the BLE scan you already do.

constructiveAlternatives.title: An optional, opt-in social-graph sanity check at re-meeting (TOFU + later in-person confirm)
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: OOB MITM with no later detection (the no-server key-transparency gap)
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Since there is no server for key transparency, use the one channel you do have: subsequent in-person meetings. On every physical co-location with a Verified contact (detected by both apps being in BLE range AND a quick re-tap), silently re-run the SAS check in the background and confirm the pinned key still matches; if it ever doesn't, raise the red 'key changed' state. This gives a poor-man's continuous key transparency that needs no infrastructure and exploits the fact that gathering-scope users physically re-meet. Strictly opt-in and local; no graph leaves the device.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Provides the missing 'fake-key attacks get detected eventually' property that Trail of Bits says serverless apps lack, using only physical re-meeting.
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Only helps for contacts you re-meet in person; adds a background SAS recompute; needs careful UX so a benign rotation isn't alarming.

creativeIdeas: Reframe the entire pitch around the HONEST core metaphor and make honesty the brand. 'A message in a bottle' is perfect precisely because everyone knows a bottle can wash up anywhere and anyone can read it if they find it — lean into that: 'We hide WHO threw the bottle and WHO it's for. We can't stop the finder from keeping it.' The metaphor itself sets correct expectations if you don't oversell it.

Ship a built-in, in-app 'Red Team Reality' mode: a one-tap simulation that shows the user, on their own phone, what an attacker sees (just blobs), what a screenshotting recipient keeps (everything), and what a seized phone reveals (nothing if locked + encrypted-at-rest). Teaching the threat model experientially beats any warning dialog and turns the limitations into a trust-building feature.

Treat the cold-start problem as event-organizing, not virality: provide organizers a 'gathering kit' (poster QR, a pre-shared event contact bundle, a 'before you come, install this' flow). The unit of adoption is a planned gathering, which matches Invariant 7's scope exactly — you don't need metropolis virality, you need 200 people in one square on one afternoon, which is an organizing problem with known solutions.

Make 'Released to the soup' a deliberately humble, almost poetic send confirmation that teaches the no-delivery-guarantee truth: a small animation of a bottle dropping into water, with copy 'Out there now. It may reach Ana, or quietly expire. You won't be told which unless she replies.' This sets the best-effort expectation emotionally instead of via a disclaimer.

Borrow aviation/medical 'forcing functions': for the highest-risk action (sending to an UNVERIFIED contact), require the user to actively acknowledge a single concrete sentence ('I have NOT verified Ana in person; a stranger could be reading this') rather than a generic 'OK' — concrete, specific acknowledgments are far less tap-throughable than generic ones.

Offer a 'paranoia budget' settings preset spectrum (Casual / Protest / Hostile) that bundles TTL, retention, prekey-strictness, duress, and warning verbosity into three honestly-described profiles, so users don't have to understand three knobs + prekeys + duress individually. Each profile screen states plainly what it does and does not protect against.

Use the OOB-only constraint as a feature for verification: since you must meet in person anyway, gamify/normalize the in-person SAS check the way Threema's QR-scan or Signal's 'mark verified' could but doesn't — make 'we verified in person' a small social ritual (matching emoji handshake) so the secure ceremony becomes the norm, not the 14%-completed chore.

techniques.name: Trail of Bits — 'Building secure messaging is hard: bitchat' (2025)
techniques.relevance: Directly analogous serverless-mesh app; documents the marketing-vs-reality gap (Dorsey: 'not robust' while floating protest use), the no-key-transparency-without-a-server problem, and the OOB-verification usability ceiling. Read it as a checklist of what NOT to repeat. (Verified via fetch.)

techniques.name: Signal authentication-ceremony usability studies (Vaziripour et al. 'Action Needed!'; Schröder et al. 'When Signal hits the fan', NDSS workshop)
techniques.relevance: Empirical 13-14% ceremony-completion rates justify replacing one-way fingerprint comparison with a mutual emoji/word SAS glance. Numbers from search; treat exact figures as approximate until you read the papers.

techniques.name: Word/emoji-based SAS and fingerprint-length usability papers (arXiv 2306.04574 'Effect of Length on Key Fingerprint Verification'; arXiv 2106.01131 'Visual and Verbal Verification of Word-based Key Fingerprints')
techniques.relevance: Evidence base for choosing short word/emoji SAS over long digit strings, and verbal-vs-visual tradeoffs, for the mutual-presence ceremony.

techniques.name: Random Oracle blog — 'Design considerations for a duress PIN' (parts I & II, 2021)
techniques.relevance: Concrete reasoning on why a duress mode whose existence is known undermines deniability, and what plausibility actually requires — directly informs the hidden-volume/decoy-identity redesign of panic/duress.

techniques.name: DataLair / hidden-volume deniable storage against multi-snapshot adversaries (arXiv 1706.10276); TrueCrypt/VeraCrypt hidden volumes
techniques.relevance: Prior art for the decoy-identity-on-duress and indistinguishable-at-rest seen-record; also the caveat that multi-snapshot forensics can defeat naive hidden volumes — informs honest limits.

techniques.name: Forensic analysis of ephemeral messaging apps (LJMU researchonline 20016)
techniques.relevance: Documents that 'disappearing' messages routinely persist in OS caches, notification logs, and backups — evidence for the truth-in-labeling cards and for not claiming recipient-side deletion.

techniques.name: FireChat Hong Kong post-mortems (Cornell INFO2040 blog; The Conversation 2014)
techniques.relevance: The canonical mesh-messenger adoption case: usage was largely LTE-backed, pure-mesh lagged at density, mobility broke links — grounds the cold-start/standby and event-seeding recommendations and the honest scope warning.

techniques.name: SOAP: A Social Authentication Protocol (arXiv 2402.03199); PakeMail/PAKE-based pairing (arXiv 2107.06090)
techniques.relevance: Alternatives/augmentations to manual OOB comparison — low-entropy shared-secret PAKE binding is exactly the 'type the same word' hardening in the mutual ceremony; SOAP shows automated social verification, though it assumes online identities meldingx lacks (note the mismatch).

techniques.name: ACLU — 'Disappearing Messages Don't Work — And They're Great' (2021)
techniques.relevance: A nuanced public framing of ephemerality's real (limited) value — a model for honest, non-fearmongering copy that still motivates the feature.

techniques.name: Briar / Bramble (already in spec §14)
techniques.relevance: Their conservative, audited UX around contact verification and Tor rendezvous is the closest good-practice analog; worth mining their pairing flow specifically for the in-person ceremony, not just the transport.

analyses.dimension: BLUE-SKY — Cross-cutting creative architecture: single primitives that solve multiple open problems at once (anti-flood + anonymity + deliverability + deletion), preserving all seven design invariants.
analyses.assessment: The v0.2 design is unusually honest and the "uniform soup" core is internally consistent: pure flooding genuinely is the cleanest way to get originate/relay indistinguishability, and the team has correctly identified that the real open problems are economic and statistical, not cryptographic. The crypto envelope is sound. Where the design is weakest is exactly where it admits to being weakest, and those weaknesses are CORRELATED in a way the spec treats as separate. Notice the pattern: anti-flood is unsolved because there is no identity to meter; relay-retention game theory is unsolved because there is no identity to incentivize; cover-traffic realism is hard because the system has no shared notion of "what normal looks like"; deliverability craters at scale because flooding has no gradient and the buffer is a flat undifferentiated bag; eviction-under-attack is hard because the buffer has no per-blob trust signal. Every one of these is a symptom of the same root cause: the wire carries NO state that a node can reason about except "this is a fixed-size blob." The blue-sky opportunity is to add ONE class of wire-visible, identity-free, anonymity-preserving signal that simultaneously meters minting (anti-flood), creates a soft non-routing gradient (deliverability), gives eviction a trust axis (eviction), and is itself the cover-traffic clock (anonymity). The biggest risk in this seat is that any such signal, if it correlates to source, sender, or recipient, instantly breaks invariants 2-4 - so the discipline below is to keep every added signal a function of the BLOB and of GLOBAL or LOCAL-NEIGHBORHOOD state, never of an endpoint. Three of my five alternatives meet that bar cleanly; one bends an invariant and is labeled; the gradient idea is the riskiest and I give a soul-preserving fallback for it.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Replace PoW with a per-blob Verifiable Delay Function (sequential time-lock), making the death-clock and the admission-cost the SAME primitive
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Open problems (a) anonymous rate-limiting, (f) eviction under adaptive attack, (c) cover-traffic decoupling; also fixes the PoW origination-timing tell and the 'defender = weakest battery, attacker = mains power' asymmetry.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: Today PoW and TTL are two unrelated things: PoW is a parallelizable hash race (so botnets/GPUs win 10^4-10^8x) and TTL is a signed timestamp a node trusts on faith. Fuse them. Require each blob to carry a Wesolowski/Pietrzak VDF proof (repeated squaring in an RSA or class group, see arxiv 2211.08162 / eprint 2025/1782) over its immutable core. A VDF is INHERENTLY SEQUENTIAL - a $10M ASIC computes it at most ~constant-factor faster than a phone, not 10^8x faster, because you cannot parallelize squaring. That single property kills the core PoW critique: the attacker's mains-power advantage collapses to a small constant. Crucially the VDF's iteration count IS a verifiable wall-clock floor: a blob proving N squarings could not have existed more than N/(max-squaring-rate) ago, giving every node a TRUSTLESS lower bound on age that complements the signed creation-timestamp (a relay can lie about 'fresh' in a refresh-TTL attack, but it cannot fake having done future sequential work). Mint cost and age-proof become one artifact. Cover blobs run the same VDF, so minting cost is identical for real and dummy (uniformity preserved). Difficulty still couples to local congestion as in the spec.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Collapses the attacker's compute asymmetry from 10^8x to a small constant - the single biggest unaddressed hole in section 8. Gives a trustless age floor that defends the immortal-message / refresh-TTL attack structurally rather than by honest-node convention. Unifies two subsystems into one auditable primitive. (Preserves all invariants: VDF proof is a function of the blob immutable core only, no endpoint identity, no routing, identical for real/cover/relayed, fully offline with class-group params.)
constructiveAlternatives.cost: VDF verification is cheap (ms) but PROOF GENERATION costs real phone time/battery per minted blob; this caps a benign user's send rate, which is arguably the point but must be tuned so a 1-minute-TTL 'right next to me' message isn't gated by a 30s VDF. Class-group VDFs avoid RSA trusted setup but are heavier to verify (prefer them - no ceremony for an offline system). Argon2-style memory-hardness is lost, so a tiny-RAM ASIC is fine - acceptable because sequentiality, not memory, is now the lever.

constructiveAlternatives.title: Rateless-IBLT / minisketch set reconciliation as the gossip layer - turns 'have/want Bloom' into near-optimal-bandwidth, attack-resistant exchange that doubles as the per-neighbor abuse meter
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Open problems (e) trial-decrypt cost at volume, (f)/section-8 per-neighbor link caps and fast-drop of bad neighbors, deliverability efficiency under churn, and battery (fewer redundant transfers).
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: The roadmap says 'Bloom have/want gossip.' Bloom summary vectors are the classic DTN approach (arxiv 1208.3871) but cost bandwidth proportional to buffer size and leak nothing useful for abuse detection. Swap in Rateless IBLT (SIGCOMM 2024, arxiv 2402.02668) or minisketch (Blockstream, used in Bitcoin Erlay). Two neighbors reconcile their blob-ID sets in bandwidth proportional to the SET DIFFERENCE, not the set size - so a phone holding 1M blobs that meets a phone with the same 1M minus 50 exchanges ~50-blobs-worth of sketch, not a megabyte Bloom. Three compounding wins: (1) BLE bandwidth is the binding constraint in a dense crowd; this is the difference between converging and not. (2) The sketch exchange is itself the per-neighbor abuse signal - a neighbor whose claimed sketch never decodes, or who always claims to 'want' everything (a sink trying to harvest the whole soup), is detectable and fast-droppable WITHOUT any identity, satisfying section-8's link-layer goal on a cryptographic rather than heuristic basis. (3) Because you only PULL blobs you provably lack, you trial-decrypt each distinct blob exactly once across your lifetime AND stop re-pulling churned blobs, directly cutting (e).
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Likely the highest ROI item here: drop-in for an already-planned component, battle-tested in Bitcoin, and it simultaneously improves deliverability, battery, trial-decrypt cost, AND gives anti-abuse a principled hook. Solves four section-13 items with one library. (Preserves all invariants: sketches are over opaque blob-IDs already public on the wire; reconciliation is symmetric, link-local, no routing, no destination - a have/want set is not a path.)
constructiveAlternatives.cost: Minisketch CPU grows with difference size (fine for slow-diverging mesh, bad after a long disconnection - mitigate by falling back to Bloom when the difference is huge, as Rateless IBLT does natively). Implementation complexity higher than a Bloom filter. A malicious neighbor can poison a sketch to waste your decode CPU - bound decode attempts per neighbor per window (already doing this for bytes).

constructiveAlternatives.title: Loopix-style Poisson loop traffic as the cover-traffic engine AND the active-delete cover AND a passive reachability/health sensor
constructiveAlternatives.addresses: Open problems (c) cheap realistic cover traffic, (d) active-delete residual, plus section-10's 'constant-rate cover is itself a discriminator' and the density-aware UX warning; partially (h) sparse-reach detection.
constructiveAlternatives.howItWorks: The spec's cover model is 'first-emission dummies that match bursty/diurnal human distribution' - but it has no concrete generator and admits constant-rate cover is a tell. Loopix (arxiv 1703.00536) solved exactly this for mixnets: every client emits THREE indistinguishable Poisson streams - real, drop-cover, and LOOP messages addressed back to itself. Port the loop concept: each phone periodically seals a blob TO ITSELF (trivial - it has its own pubkey) with a short TTL and drops it in the soup. Four payoffs from one mechanism. (1) Cover realism: real sends, drop-cover, and self-loops are byte-identical and all Poisson, so the aggregate is a clean Poisson process - provably indistinguishable origination, stronger than 'match diurnal' hand-waving. (2) Reachability sensor WITHOUT routing: if your self-loop comes BACK to you (a neighbor carried it and re-broadcast it into range later), you have evidence the local mesh is live and round-tripping; if loops never return, you're in a sparse/percolation-failure regime - powering the section-10 density-aware UX warning with a real signal. (3) Active-delete cover: a real delete can't hide among decoy deletes (only sender+recipient can mint one) - but the SENDER broadcasting 'secret' after random delay looks like 'a phone deleting its own message,' and if every phone is constantly expiring its own self-loops, 'a phone's blob just went away' is background noise, shrinking the (d) residual. (4) Battery floor: loop rate IS the charge-independent uniformity floor section-10 asks for.
constructiveAlternatives.benefit: Replaces hand-wavy cover with a primitive that has formal anonymity analysis (Loopix), and extracts a free offline reachability sensor and active-delete camouflage from the same packets. Turns three soft TODO items into one mechanism with a paper behind it. (Preserves all invariants: self-loops are sealed to your OWN key, carry no third party, propagate identically, need no internet, no routing.)
